**4e.** Check excursions: working side — preserve the working cusp contacts (cuspid-protected occlusion or bilateral balanced as planned). Balancing side — remove any interferences on the non-working side.
**4f.** Protrusion — anteriors should contact, posteriors should disocclude. Adjust any posterior contact in protrusion.
**5a.** Smooth all ground surfaces with a rubber polishing point.
**5b.** Re-polish on the lab lathe — pumice with a rag wheel, then high-shine with a tin oxide wet wheel.
**5c.** Verify no rough edges remain — run a finger over the occlusal surfaces, check for cusp tip irregularities.
**5d.** Re-deliver to the patient. Re-check MIP intraorally with articulating paper.
**5e.** Confirm patient is comfortable, give POI instructions, schedule 24-hour and 1-week follow-up.

blocks.title: Why bother with a clinical remount?
blocks.body: Processing changes the occlusion. A wax try-in that occluded perfectly will occlude slightly differently after flasking, packing, curing, and deflasking — typical change is 50–200 µm of vertical loss and lateral shift of cusp-fossa relationships. Without a clinical remount, this discrepancy is felt by the patient as 'high spots' that they grind into over the first 2–4 weeks of wear, creating soreness and accelerated wear. A 30-minute clinical remount eliminates the discrepancy at delivery. Skipping it is the single most common preventable post-delivery complaint.

blocks.title: Issues
blocks.body: **Bite record distorts under closure:** material was too thick. Re-take with a pea-sized amount of material. **Casts don't seat into the bite record:** the patient closed in a different position than the wax try-in record — this is the entire point of clinical remount. Split-mount and re-pour to the new record. **Posterior teeth grind to nothing during selective adjustment:** you removed too much. Re-do the setup at the lab — a denture with shallow cusps and minimal posterior contact will accelerate ridge resorption. **Patient reports 'high spot' returning at 1-week recall:** soft-tissue swelling has resolved, allowing the denture to seat further than at delivery, revealing new contacts. Re-do selective grinding at this visit.

blocks.text: *Where this fits in the workflow:* You arrive at clinical remount after processing and immediately before the patient's first day of denture wear. The 24-hour and 1-week follow-up visits use the post-delivery complaints chapter to diagnose any complaints that emerge. Clinical remount is the bridge between fabrication and real-world function — a denture delivered without it will require more adjustment visits over the first month than one delivered with it.*

chapters.title: Attachment systems for implant overdentures + tooth-supported overdentures

blocks.text: Overdentures retain via attachments rather than tissue suction. The choice of attachment determines retention force, ease of replacement, and the chairside maintenance schedule. Five systems dominate clinical practice; each has its place and none is universally best.
Locator (the current default for 2-implant mandibular overdenture)

items: **Construction:** Patrix (male) screws into the implant; matrix (female nylon insert) sits in the denture base. Multiple retention strengths via colored inserts (clear, pink, blue, green, red — weakest to strongest).
**Retention range:** 1.5–5 lb (mild to extra-strong).
**Pickup:** at delivery or chairside reline. Block out the patrix path with the supplied black processing inserts during pickup so the nylon doesn't get caught in PMMA.

**Replacement schedule:** nylon inserts wear and lose retention; replace every 6–12 months on average heavy use, 12–24 months light use. Patient can be taught self-replacement with proper instruction.
**Strengths:** low vertical profile (works in tight inter-arch space), forgiving of divergent implant angulations (up to 40° between two implants), retention adjustable.
**Weaknesses:** nylon inserts wear out (recurring maintenance cost), can over-retain causing housing fracture if too strong an insert is used.

blocks.text: Ball attachment (older standard, still in use)

items: **Construction:** Ball patrix screws into the implant; matrix is a metal housing with a retentive cap that grips the ball.
**Retention range:** 2–4 lb. Adjusted by squeezing the metal cap closer to the ball (more retention) or spreading it (less).
**Pickup:** at delivery with cold-cure PMMA around the metal housings.
**Strengths:** simpler than Locator, cheaper per attachment, durable metal-on-metal connection.

**Weaknesses:** higher vertical profile (needs more inter-arch space), less forgiving of angulation differences (>10° between implants causes binding), retention adjustment is mechanical (squeeze) and irreversible.
**Construction:** A bar connects 2 or more implants. Clips in the denture base engage the bar for retention.
**Retention range:** Variable; typically 4–8 lb (strong).
**Indications:** 4+ implants, severely resorbed ridge where stress distribution matters, cases requiring rigid splinting of implants.
**Pickup:** lab-fabricated, picked up in the denture at delivery.
**Strengths:** splints implants together (load distribution), high retention, durable.

**Weaknesses:** lab cost (custom bar fabrication), takes more inter-arch space than studs (Locator/ball), more difficult to clean (requires interproximal brushes around the bar), patient must remove the prosthesis daily to clean the bar.
**Construction:** A magnetic keeper (patrix) is attached to the implant abutment or root cap. The denture contains a complementary magnet (matrix).
**Retention range:** 1–2 lb (low).
**Indications:** Limited use — patients with very low dexterity who cannot manage higher retention forces, or as backup retention for tissue-supported dentures with sound canine roots.
**Strengths:** Easy seat/release. Low force on abutments.
**Weaknesses:** Corrodes in oral environment over years (replace at 5–7 years). Low retention compared to other systems; not adequate for primary retention on most cases.
**Construction:** Stud patrix on the implant; rubber O-ring or plastic insert in the denture matrix.
**Retention range:** Adjustable via insert color (similar to Locator).
**Indications:** Older or budget alternative to Locator. Some practices prefer the simpler design.
**Strengths:** Inexpensive, simple chairside replacement of inserts.
**Weaknesses:** Less forgiving of angulation than Locator. Some studies show shorter insert lifespan.
**2-implant mandibular overdenture with parallel implants in canine regions:** Locator (default) or ball.
**2-implant mandibular with >10° angulation between implants:** Locator (handles up to 40° divergence).
**4-implant overdenture or severely resorbed mandible:** Bar attachment.
**Patient with very low dexterity:** Locator with weakest nylon (clear), magnet attachments if needed.
**Patient with retained natural canine roots (tooth-supported overdenture):** Custom-cast copings + ball or O-ring attachments. Locators can fit on root copings too.

blocks.title: The maintenance reality
blocks.body: Implant overdentures have a hidden chairside-time cost: insert replacement, housing relines, attachment cleaning. Expect a recall visit every 6–12 months for these patients. Budget 20–30 minutes per visit for routine attachment maintenance. The patient should be counseled at delivery that this is part of owning an implant overdenture; surprise charges at year 1 cause complaints.

blocks.title: Issues
blocks.body: **Insert tears during seating:** the patrix has a sharp edge (worn) or the insert size is wrong. Replace with new insert; if recurs, replace the patrix. **Denture rotates around one attachment:** the other attachment is non-functional (worn insert, housing crack, abutment screw loose). Diagnose and fix the non-functional side. **Retention force decreases over months:** normal nylon wear. Replace inserts with same color first; only escalate to higher retention if patient reports inadequacy. **Patient cannot seat denture independently:** retention is too high for their dexterity. Step down insert color one level.

blocks.text: *Where this fits in the workflow:* Used in cd-implant-supported-lower, cd-iod-canine-roots, cross-cd-iod-implants, and rpd-implant-hybrid pathways. Pickup of attachments references (processing) and (reline/rebase/repair) for the chairside technique; ongoing maintenance lives in (complaints).*

Tissue conditioners are soft, pliable materials applied to the intaglio surface of a denture chairside to relieve soreness, condition irritated tissue before final impression, or serve as an interim liner between fabrication phases. They're not the same as a lab-cured permanent soft liner — these are short-term materials measured in weeks, not years.

items: **Coe Comfort (GC America):** tissue conditioner per the clinic Materials reference. Powder + liquid, mixed chairside. Pliable for ~4–6 weeks. The clinic default for IID transition relines + chronic soreness conditioning.
**Coe Soft (GC America):** soft reline material per the clinic Materials reference. Use when a longer-lasting soft surface is needed (1–3 months pliability).
**GC Reline:** hard reline material per the clinic Materials reference. Used for definitive chairside hard reline.
**Visco-gel / Lynal (general knowledge, not on clinic Materials reference):** historical tissue conditioners similar to Coe Comfort. Clinic may differ from these specific brands.

**Patient with acute soreness in a single area (1-week complaint):** Coe-Soft or Lynal, spot-applied with PIP-identification first to localize the area. Often resolves the spot soreness in 24–48 hours.

**IID patient at 2-week post-op:** Coe-Soft or COE-Comfort full reline of the IID intaglio. Conditions the tissue as the ridge remodels; replaced every 3–4 weeks until ready for hard reline at 3–6 months.

**Patient with poor tissue health (chronic inflammation under existing denture):** apply tissue conditioner full intaglio, replace weekly for 2–3 weeks to allow tissue to heal before final impression for new denture or reline.
**Bridge between failed denture and new fabrication:** tissue conditioner reline of the failed denture buys 4–6 weeks of comfort while the new prosthesis is fabricated.
**1.** Examine the tissue. Identify red areas, ulcerations, hyperplasia. Photograph if extensive.
**2.** Adjust the denture first — relieve any sharp edges, polish over-extended flanges. Apply PIP to identify pressure spots.
**3.** Clean the denture intaglio. The conditioner won't bond to a contaminated surface.
**4.** Mix the conditioner per manufacturer ratio (typically 1:1 powder:liquid by volume; check the package).
**5.** Apply a thin even layer to the intaglio with a spatula or your finger (wear gloves).
**6.** Seat the denture in the patient's mouth with light finger pressure. Have the patient close gently into MIP for 30 seconds, then open.
**7.** Wait for initial set per material (90 sec – 3 min). Remove the denture.
**8.** Trim excess conditioner from the flange borders with a scalpel or scissors. Smooth borders.
**9.** Re-seat. Verify retention, occlusion is still acceptable. Counsel the patient.
Wear continuously for the first 24 hours, including sleeping, then return to normal day-wear schedule.
Soak in water (NOT denture cleaner) when out of mouth — denture cleaner discolors the soft material.
Clean with a soft brush + mild soap (NOT toothpaste — abrasive).
Replace every 2–4 weeks for routine use, sooner if it becomes hard, odorous, or torn.
Soreness should improve within 24–48 hours; if not, return for re-evaluation.

blocks.title: Tissue conditioner vs lab-cured soft liner — when to escalate
blocks.body: Tissue conditioner is a TEMPORARY material. Use 2–4 weeks; replace as needed. If a patient continues to need tissue conditioner reapplied month after month, the underlying problem is not being addressed — usually it's chronic ridge inflammation that needs a permanent solution. Options: lab-cured soft liner (Permasoft, Molloplast-B; 1–2 years of life), permanent reline, or denture remake. Don't let a patient become dependent on weekly tissue conditioner — investigate the root cause (parafunctional habits, occlusal scheme problem, xerostomia, ill-fitting denture).

blocks.title: Issues
blocks.body: **Conditioner won't bond to the denture intaglio:** the acrylic surface was contaminated (saliva, denture cleaner residue). Clean with alcohol; re-roughen the intaglio lightly with a slow-speed bur if needed; re-apply. **Conditioner remains pliable for far longer than expected (months):** patient is keeping the denture wet continuously, which retards the firming process. Normal — replace per schedule regardless. **Patient develops a yeast infection (denture stomatitis) while wearing the liner:** the soft conditioner harbors candida. Treat with antifungal (nystatin oral suspension), replace the conditioner, counsel improved hygiene. **The denture feels too tight after liner application:** the liner thickness exceeded the available space, often because the patient didn't fully seat during initial application. Remove the liner and re-apply with better seating technique.

blocks.text: *Where this fits in the workflow:* Tissue conditioners are referenced in cd-iid (transition between surgical and final reline), cd-xerostomic (comfort liner for sensitive tissues), repair-loose-cd (interim solution while diagnosing the underlying loose-denture cause), and any pathway involving a denture being worn while a new one is fabricated. Always paired with the next-step plan — the conditioner buys time, doesn't solve.*

parts.label: All chapters
parts.tagline: Generated from build_rpd_expandables.py on the removable partial dentures build script.

chapters.title: How to take a facebow record

blocks.text: A facebow captures the spatial relationship between the maxillary arch and the patient's hinge axis (transverse axis of the TMJ). With that record, the lab can mount the maxillary cast on the articulator in the same position the maxilla actually sits in the patient — which means excursive movements on the articulator approximate the patient's real excursive movements.

items: **Denar Slidematic facebow** (standard) with all joints intact + tightened-finger-tight at default.
**Bite fork** matched to the facebow model.
**Regisil PVS** cartridge + dispenser gun + fresh mixing tip (standard bite-reg material).
Cotton rolls (for fork stabilization during set).
Marker (label fork orientation if needed for the lab).
**Patient prep:** seated upright, head firmly against the headrest, eyes on a fixed point straight ahead (not looking at the dentist), shoulders relaxed.
Pump pea-size discard from Regisil PVS cartridge.
Attach fresh mixing tip.
Apply Regisil to **upper surface of the fork**: cover palatal area + lingual halves of posterior teeth. You want firm engagement of the maxillary arch, not just incisal edges.
**Working time: ~45 sec** from start of dispensing.
Insert fork into patient's mouth, Regisil-side up against maxillary teeth.
Push gently upward so PVS captures cusp tips + palatal anatomy.
**Have patient bite gently on the back of the fork** using cotton rolls or a finger rest to stabilize.
Fork should NOT rock. If it rocks, reseat with more material.
**Hold steady until full set** — Regisil PVS typically 2–3 min (verify with cartridge).
Once Regisil is fully set: slide the facebow's transfer arm onto the bite fork's stem.
Tighten the transfer-arm locking knob finger-tight.
Position the earpieces of the facebow into the patient's external auditory meatus.
**BOTH earpieces seated symmetrically** — uneven seating = asymmetric mounting (canted maxillary cast).
Eyeball the patient from the front: the bow should appear level across the face.
Find the patient's **right infraorbital notch** (the small depression under the orbital rim on the patient's right side — your left when facing them).
Adjust the orbital pointer to touch this landmark.
The bow should now be roughly parallel to the Frankfort horizontal plane.
Tighten the top central knob.
Tighten each side knob.
Tighten the transfer-arm knob if not already.
Once locked, the bow holds the bite fork in its exact transferred position.
**Do not loosen anything from here on.**
Loosen the bite fork from the patient's mouth (the set Regisil stays on the fork).
Carefully remove the entire facebow assembly from the patient as a unit.
Carry the locked facebow assembly to the lab.
**Do NOT change any joint positions in transit.** If a knob loosens, the mounting is invalid.

blocks.title: Mistakes to avoid
blocks.body: **Asymmetric earpiece engagement** is the most common error. Press both earpieces in firmly and check the bow is level (eyeball the patient from the front). **Locking the bow before PVS has fully set.** Wait for full polymerization (~2–3 minutes) before transferring — if the bite reg is still soft, the fork shifts when you torque the locking knobs and the maxillary cast mounts off-axis. **Orbital pointer at the wrong landmark.** It goes on the LEFT (patient's right) infraorbital notch when you're standing in front of the patient — sloppy aiming is the #1 cause of canted mountings.

chapters.title: How to mount diagnostic casts on Denar 320

blocks.text: Mounting is the step that converts your impressions into a 3D model of the patient's static + dynamic occlusion. mounting required for RPD diagnosis — the impressions lecture (4A) calls it "absolutely necessary", a hard gate before any design work. Plan a 90-minute lab session.

items: **Denar 320 articulator** (the standard for partials).
**Mounting plates** (top + bottom) — magnetic or screw-on depending on the unit. Wipe clean with alcohol, dry.
**Both poured casts** (maxillary + mandibular) — trimmed flat on the bottom, dried at least 24 hours, no fractured teeth or short borders.
**Mounting stone** (Type III, fast-set preferred, e.g. Snap-Stone or Mounting Stone) + mixing bowl + spatula + water measure.
**Vaseline / petroleum jelly** (light coat under the mounting plate for easier remount if needed).
**Locked face-bow assembly** from the patient (Whip-Mix or Denar style) with bite fork and reference rod still in position from the patient appointment.
**Regisil PVS bite registration** (in MI, taken at the patient appointment, verified articulated cleanly without rocking).
**Hot glue gun** or sticky wax (to tack the cast bases to the mounting stone for the second arch).
**Damp paper towel** to wipe stone splatter; small brush for cleaning excess stone after set.
Both casts trimmed: bottom flat, sides straight, height of base ~25 mm (Pindex base or hand-trimmed). Land area visible all around.
Articulator opened, centric latch confirmed working, incisal pin free to slide.
Mounting plates clean and seated. Verify the magnets engage (or screws tight).
Bench area cleared and at eye level. Good task lighting.
Stone bowl + spatula ready, water pre-measured (typically 30 mL per 100 g of mounting stone — check the bag).
Face-bow assembly verified intact: bite fork still in original position, locking screws tight, no shifts since the patient appointment.

blocks.text: Step 1 — Mount maxillary cast via face-bow (25 min)

items: **1a.** Attach the face-bow's transfer jig to the Denar's mounting jig (the metal arm that hangs over the upper mounting plate). The bite fork now positions the maxillary cast in the same spatial position the maxilla sits in the patient.
**1b.** Drop the maxillary cast onto the bite fork — the cast's tooth occlusal surfaces fit into the bite-fork registration material (the bite registration that was taken at the patient visit).
**1c.** Confirm the cast seats fully and doesn't rock. Don't proceed if there's rock — either the cast wasn't trimmed right or the bite fork registration was disturbed in transit. Reseat or repour.
**1d.** Mix the mounting stone (30 mL water per 100 g stone for typical Snap-Stone; consistency should be like soft frosting, holds a peak but flows when nudged).
**1e.** Apply stone to the underside of the maxillary cast (covering the upper surface of the cast base). Apply matching stone to the top of the articulator's upper mounting plate.
**1f.** Bring the mounting plate down onto the cast. The stone bridges between cast and plate. Wipe excess off the sides with a damp gauze.
**1g.** Let set per manufacturer timing — typically 5 min for working strength, 30 min for full set. Don't disturb during the first 5 min.
**1h.** After 5 min, remove the face-bow assembly (the bite fork stays attached to the face-bow; the cast is now anchored to the articulator).

blocks.text: Step 2 — Mount mandibular cast via bite registration (25 min)

items: **2a.** Invert the articulator (Denar 320 is designed to invert — the upper arm becomes the lower for mounting purposes, or you mount with the articulator right-side up depending on faculty preference).

**2b.** Seat the Regisil PVS bite registration between the maxillary cast (already mounted) and the unseated mandibular cast. The mandibular cast's teeth must fit into the indentations in the bite reg.
**2c.** Confirm: the bite reg is fully seated on both arches, no rock, no gap. The condylar elements on the articulator should sit against the centric stop (the rear-most position).
**2d.** Mix a fresh batch of mounting stone (same ratio).
**2e.** Apply stone to the underside of the mandibular cast (now facing up because the articulator is inverted) and to the underside of the lower mounting plate.
**2f.** Close the articulator gently — let the casts find their position in the bite reg, then hold them lightly against the stone. Don't force or you'll squeeze the bite reg.

**2g.** Verify by eye: maxillary cast and mandibular cast are in proper relationship through the bite reg. Incisal pin contacts the table (if pre-set to the correct VDO; for diagnostic mounting, the incisal pin position isn't critical — just confirm closure).
**2h.** Let set 5 min undisturbed, 30 min for full set.
**3a.** Remove the bite reg. Hold the articulator at the centric stop.
**3b. The natural teeth on both casts should touch each other simultaneously**, matching the patient's MI.
**3c.** Open and close several times. Watch for: do the same teeth contact each time? Do contacts come in evenly, or does one tooth strike first?

**3d.** Check excursive movements: move the articulator into right lateral, then left lateral, then protrusive. Movements should be smooth without binding (assuming Denar default settings: condylar inclination 30°, immediate side shift 0 mm).
**3e.** Verify condylar settings: condylar inclination 30° (default), Bennett angle / progressive side shift per the model's standard, immediate side shift 0 mm. Confirm with faculty if uncertain.

blocks.title: Verify the mounting before designing
blocks.body: Hold the articulator at the centric stop — the natural teeth on both casts should touch each other simultaneously, matching the patient's MI. If they don't, the bite reg was wrong, the maxillary was mounted off, or both. **Don't proceed to surveying / design until the mounting is correct.**

blocks.title: Issues
blocks.body: **Cast rocks on bite-fork registration during Step 1:** the bite-fork bite registration was deformed or the cast doesn't fit. Re-do the face-bow record on the patient if possible; if not, mount mandibular first with a hand-articulated approach and accept the loss of face-bow data. **Mounting stone too runny:** you used too much water. Pour out, remix at correct ratio. Runny stone slumps and the casts shift before set. **Mounting stone too thick:** too little water; stone doesn't bond well to cast or plate. Same fix — pour out, remix. **Natural teeth don't simultaneously contact at centric stop after Step 3:** bite registration was off or the mandibular cast mounted at a slightly different position than the patient's MI. Re-take the bite reg on the patient and re-mount the mandibular cast (the maxillary mount via face-bow stays). **One side of the cast lifted off the mounting plate during set:** insufficient stone bonding. Hot-glue the cast to a fresh stone mix and re-mount that side only. **Articulator opens with a click or grinding sound:** excess mounting stone has gotten into the condylar elements. Clean carefully with a small brush + alcohol. If stone has set in the condyle, the articulator needs servicing.

blocks.text: Surveying is the procedure that converts a cast into a designable thing. You're determining: (a) the path of insertion the partial will take, (b) where the height of contour falls on each potential abutment, (c) which undercuts are usable for retention, (d) which undercuts will block the framework and need to be blocked out with wax before duplicating. Done on the diagnostic cast first, repeated on the master cast. Plan a 60–90 min lab session.

items: **Dental surveyor** (the pre-clinic lab has the Ney parallelometer or equivalent — verify which model you have).
**Mounted diagnostic cast** (poured + trimmed, mounted on Denar 320 if available, dried fully).
**Analyzing rod** (graphite tip) for finding + marking HOC.
**Undercut gauges:** 0.01", 0.02", 0.03" (different depth probes for different clasp materials).
**Wax knife / trimmer tip** for the surveyor — shaves wax block-out parallel to path of insertion.
**Surveying lead / red pencil** for transferring HOC line to the cast.
**Hard baseplate wax** for blocking out unwanted undercuts.
**Soft pencil + small carbide pencil sharpener** for marking on the cast.
**Tripod marker** (or a permanent pencil) for scribing reference dots.
**the Lab 4 Design Form** (paper or digital) to record findings.
**Vertical spindle (analyzing rod):** the rod that travels straight up and down. Its lowest end is the graphite tip you mark with.
**Horizontal platform / cast holder:** the tilt-table where the cast sits. You can tilt the cast on this platform to change the apparent path of insertion.
**Surveyor base:** the heavy stationary base.
**Tools that go into the vertical spindle (interchangeable):** analyzing rod (graphite tip), undercut gauges, wax knife, surveying lead / marker.
Verify the cast is fully dry (wet cast slides on the surveyor platform).
Surveyor on a level bench, no wobble.
Analyzing rod tip sharp (graphite). Replace if dull — you can't read HOC lines that aren't crisp.
Cast oriented on the platform: occlusal plane roughly parallel to the platform initially. You'll adjust tilt during Step 2.
Lab 4 Design Form on the bench, ready to fill in.

blocks.text: Step 1 — Mount the cast on the surveyor's platform (5 min)

items: **1a.** Place the cast on the horizontal platform with the occlusal plane facing up.
**1b.** Secure with the platform's clamping mechanism (or modeling clay if surveyor has no clamp).
**1c.** Verify no movement — nudge the cast lightly to confirm it's locked in.
**1d.** **start with a horizontal (level) platform** — single-level path of insertion, no tilt. Rotational paths are reserved for advanced cases.

blocks.text: Step 2 — Determine the path of insertion (15 min)

items: **2a.** Lower the analyzing rod (vertical spindle) against the buccal surface of one potential abutment. Observe where the rod contacts the tooth.
**2b.** Move the rod around the tooth's circumference at the same vertical height — identify the most prominent point (the height of contour at this path).
**2c.** Repeat for each potential abutment.
**2d.** If undercuts are accessible from the same direction on all abutments, that direction is your path of insertion. Lock the platform.

**2e. If undercuts are NOT all accessible from the same direction at horizontal:** tilt the cast (per faculty approval) to find a single position where all desired undercuts are accessible. Common tilts: anterior tilt for Kennedy I (engages distal undercuts on terminal abutments) or posterior tilt for Kennedy III/IV.
**2f.** Lock the platform once the optimal path is found. **Do not change tilt again** — from this moment, the cast represents the patient at that path of insertion.

blocks.text: Step 3 — Survey each potential abutment (15 min)

items: **3a.** Touch the graphite tip of the analyzing rod to one abutment's buccal surface near the cervical.
**3b.** Raise the rod slowly until the graphite first contacts the tooth at the height of contour. Note the height.
**3c.** Move the rod around the circumference of the tooth at the path-of-insertion direction. The graphite traces the HOC.
**3d.** Transfer the HOC line to the cast with a soft pencil (or, if the surveyor has a marking lead, scribe directly).
**3e.** Repeat for every potential abutment (every tooth that might carry a rest, clasp, or guide plane).

blocks.text: Step 4 — Identify clasp-tip positions + measure undercuts (10 min)

items: **4a.** Identify the gingival 1/3 of each abutment's clinical crown — that's the zone where clasp tips engage retention.
**4b.** Pick the surface for the clasp tip (the surface *away* from where the rest sits — opposite-direction engagement for retention).
**4c.** Switch the analyzing rod for the **0.01" undercut gauge**. The gauge tip extends 0.01" out from the vertical spindle.
**4d.** Lower the gauge along the path of insertion. When the gauge tip touches the tooth at the gingival 1/3, that's the depth point: there's **0.01" of usable undercut** at that point.

**4e.** If no 0.01" undercut exists, try the 0.02" gauge for wrought-wire clasps. If still no undercut, plan to either (1) add composite to create an undercut, (2) change path of insertion, or (3) place a survey crown.
**4f.** Record undercut location (mid-B, MB, DB, ML, DL) and depth on the Lab 4 Design Form.
**5a.** With the cast at its locked path-of-insertion position, lower the analyzing rod to the cast surface (the land area, not the tooth).
**5b.** Scribe a tripod dot at one anterior location (front of cast).
**5c.** Scribe two more tripod dots at two posterior locations (one on each side of the cast).

**5d.** These three dots are in the same Z-plane (same vertical height). They let you re-mount the cast at the same path of insertion later — critical for surveying the master cast in the same orientation.
**5e.** Note the tripod marks on the cast in pencil so they're visible during framework design.

blocks.text: Step 6 — Block out unwanted undercuts (15 min)

items: **6a.** Identify any undercut on the cast that is *not* being engaged by a clasp. These are blockers — the framework can't insert past them.
**6b.** Soften hard baseplate wax with a Bunsen flame.
**6c.** Press soft wax into the undercut area on the cast.
**6d.** Switch the spindle tool to the wax-knife / trimmer tip.
**6e.** Lower the wax trimmer along the path of insertion. The trimmer shaves the wax flush to the vertical, creating a smooth surface that the framework can slide past without catching.
**6f.** Repeat for each unwanted undercut on the cast.
**6g.** Confirm by re-lowering the analyzing rod — there should be no undercut remaining where you blocked out.

blocks.text: Step 7 — Mark proximal guide planes (5 min)

items: **7a.** Identify the proximal surfaces (mesial / distal) on each abutment that will host guide planes — typically the proximal surface facing the edentulous area.
**7b.** Mark on the cast with a soft pencil: "GP" or a shaded box on the proximal surface.
**7c.** Record on the Lab 4 Design Form: tooth #, guide plane location (M or D), planned vertical extent (typically the cervical 2/3 of the proximal surface).
**7d.** Guide planes will be prepared chairside at the tooth preparation appointment.

blocks.title: What the surveyor tells you
blocks.body: **Survey line** = height of contour at the chosen path of insertion. Above the line = suprabulge area (clasp origins live here). Below the line = infrabulge area (clasp tips engage undercuts here). **Usable undercut** = 0.01" for cast or 0.02" for wrought-wire, in the gingival 1/3, on the surface AWAY from the rest (so the clasp tip approaches from the opposite direction the rest sits). **No usable undercut** = you need to add composite to create one, OR change the path of insertion, OR plan a survey crown.

blocks.title: Issues
blocks.body: **Cast won't stay flat on the platform:** bottom of the cast wasn't trimmed flat. Trim flat on the model trimmer; re-mount. **Graphite tip leaves no mark:** the lead is dull or the cast surface is wet. Sharpen the lead; dry the cast. **Multiple potential paths of insertion all look equivalent:** consult faculty. There's almost always one path that's better — usually the one that engages the most desirable undercuts and provides better esthetics for clasps. **No usable undercut on a key abutment after exhausting tilts:** document on Lab 4 form, then either (1) plan composite addition (etch + bond + composite to create 0.01" undercut on the buccal cervical 1/3), (2) plan a survey crown, or (3) use a different abutment with a different clasp type. Discuss with faculty. **Tripod dots get smudged before transfer:** re-scribe deeper / darker. Tripod marks are critical — the master cast survey depends on them. **Block-out wax doesn't shave cleanly with the trimmer:** wax was too soft or too hard. Reheat to a soft (not liquid) consistency; reapply; try again.

blocks.text: The custom tray is built in the lab on the diagnostic cast, then used chairside at the master impression appointment. It's the most patient-specific tray you'll use — its borders are trimmed to the patient's vestibule, its spacer is calibrated to the impression material thickness, and its stops let it seat reproducibly. Plan a 60–90 min lab session.

items: **Diagnostic cast** (poured + trimmed, fully dry, no fractured teeth).
**Baseplate wax** (pink sheet wax) — 2 sheets total for teeth + tooth-supported edentulous areas; 1 sheet for distal extension areas. Pre-warm in a wax warmer or hot water bath.
**Tray resin** — stocks **Triad VLC** (light-cured) sheets typically. Acrylic resin (self-cure) is an alternative.
**Triad light-curing box** (if using Triad).
**Sharp scalpel** (#15 blade) for trimming borders.
**Wax spatula** + **Le Cron carver** for shaping.
**Acrylic burs** (carbide, cross-cut) for finishing borders.
**Sandpaper / arbor band** on lab handpiece for smoothing.
**Petroleum jelly / separator** to coat the cast (lets the tray release cleanly after cure).
**Marker** for marking borders on the cast.
Diagnostic cast is dry, trimmed, and on the bench at eye level.
Mark the border outline on the cast in pencil: 2 mm short of the deepest sulcus all around (the tray will be trimmed back to this line in Step 6).
Identify the locations for tissue stops on the cast: canine area B/L, first molar area B/L (4–5 stops total in a fully arched cast; 3 minimum).
Identify tooth-supported edentulous areas vs. distal extension areas (the wax spacer is different for each).
Pre-warm baseplate wax to soft consistency.
Triad curing unit on, ready.

blocks.text: Step 1 — Block out major undercuts (5 min)

items: **1a.** Identify major undercuts on the cast that would lock impression material in place (deep proximal undercuts on tilted teeth, deep buccal undercuts on prominent abutments).

**1b. Critical:** only block out areas where impression material would mechanically trap the tray. Do *not* block out abutment undercuts that the partial design will engage — those undercuts need to be present in the master impression so the framework can be designed to engage them.
**1c.** Press hard baseplate wax into the major undercuts; trim flush with a Le Cron.

blocks.text: Step 2 — Lay baseplate wax spacer over teeth + tooth-supported areas (10 min)

items: **2a.** Take 2 sheets of pre-warmed soft baseplate wax.
**2b.** Lay the first sheet over the teeth and tooth-supported edentulous areas. Adapt with finger pressure — firmly press into all interproximal areas and occlusal grooves.
**2c.** Lay the second sheet on top. Adapt the same way.
**2d.** Total spacer thickness: ~2 mm. This is the standoff distance for the master impression material.
**2e.** Trim the wax to match the planned tray border (2 mm short of the deepest sulcus).
**3a.** With the scalpel, cut 3×3 mm openings through the baseplate wax at the planned tissue-stop locations: canine occlusal surface B/L and first molar occlusal surface B/L.
**3b.** The openings reveal the cast directly — through these, the tray resin will contact the cast, creating positive seat stops.
**3c.** Verify the openings are small (~3×3 mm). Too large = the tray contacts cast over too much area and the spacer fails. Too small = no stop bond.

blocks.text: Step 4 — Lay single wax sheet over distal extension areas (5 min)

items: **4a.** If the case has distal extensions (Kennedy I or II), lay **only one sheet** of baseplate wax over the distal extension ridges.
**4b.** Adapt with finger pressure.
**4c.** Trim to the planned tray border.
**4d.** The single-sheet spacer (~1 mm) is intentional — distal extension impressions use heavier-body PVS for better tissue capture, and you want less standoff.

blocks.text: Step 5 — Apply tray resin (Triad VLC) (10 min)

items: **5a.** Open a fresh sheet of Triad VLC. Light-cure resin is sticky — handle on the wax-paper backing.
**5b.** Lay the Triad sheet over the entire arch, covering all wax + tissue-stop openings.
**5c.** Press firmly so the resin contacts the cast through the tissue-stop openings — you should see the resin push down through each opening.
**5d.** Pinch / mold the resin around the buccal + lingual surfaces, conforming to the wax spacer.
**5e.** Trim excess resin at the borders with a scalpel.

blocks.text: Step 6 — Trim borders + add handle / finger rests (10 min)

items: **6a.** Use the scalpel to trim the tray borders to 2 mm short of the full vestibule depth (use your pencil mark from setup).
**6b.** Student-grade rule: if the patient's tongue can lift through into the lingual sulcus, the tray border isn't getting in the way.
**6c. Anterior handle:** roll a piece of fresh Triad into a ~8–10 mm tall vertical handle. Attach to the front of the tray, parallel to the mid-sagittal plane.
**6d. Finger rests:** place two small ~5×5 mm Triad pads on top of the tray over the first molar regions B/L. These give you a place to push down without distorting the tray.
**7a.** Place the entire cast+tray assembly in the Triad light-curing box.
**7b.** Cure for **2 min** with the cameo (outer) surface up.
**7c.** Remove. Flip the tray off the cast (or remove the cast from inside the tray) carefully.
**7d.** Re-insert tray-only and cure the **intaglio (inner)** surface for 1 min.
**7e.** Tray should now be fully cured, hard, dimensionally stable.

blocks.text: Step 8 — Remove wax + final adjustment (10 min)

items: **8a.** Remove all baseplate wax from the intaglio surface of the tray. The wax has done its job (spacer for impression material).
**8b.** Verify the tissue-stop locations on the intaglio: 3 (or more) positive resin bumps that contact the cast when seated.
**8c. Final border adjustment:** remove 2 mm of resin from the borders of the intaglio to create space for the border molding compound. Use a carbide bur on the lab handpiece.
**8d.** Smooth all external surfaces with an arbor band.

**8e.** Inspect: no sharp edges, no rough spots, handle secure, finger rests secure. Test-seat on the cast — the tray should rock gently into place on the three tissue stops and rest with ~2 mm uniform clearance from the teeth + tissue everywhere else.

blocks.title: What the tissue stops do
blocks.body: Without them, the tray floats on the impression material — every time you seat it, it lands at a slightly different depth, and your impression captures slightly different anatomy. With three stops, the tray seats consistently on the same three points each time. Tissue stops should be small (3 mm × 3 mm) — just enough resin to contact the cast through the wax. Too large and they interfere with impression material flow.

blocks.title: Issues
blocks.body: **Tray rocks on the cast after curing:** tissue stops weren't seated evenly. Re-fabricate with attention to pressing all three (or more) stop openings firmly during Step 5. **Tray doesn't release from cast after cure:** didn't apply separator to the cast, or undercuts weren't blocked. Soak in warm water 5 min, pry gently with the Le Cron. Block out remaining undercuts before retrying. **Borders extended past the sulcus (too long):** trim back with the scalpel or acrylic bur. Easier to over-trim and add green stick later (border molding) than to leave the patient sore. **Borders fall short (too short):** add Triad to the existing tray and re-cure. Don't deliver a tray that misses the functional sulcus — the master impression will miss the same anatomy. **Handle breaks off when stress-tested:** the Triad bond wasn't fully cured at the handle joint. Re-attach with fresh Triad and re-cure under the light for 2 min. **Spacer wax sticks to the tray after cure:** normal — remove with the Le Cron and warm water. The tray intaglio should be smooth Triad with the tissue stop bumps visible.

blocks.text: the preferred master impression technique for ALL Kennedy classifications EXCEPT Kennedy I/II where you're using altered cast technique instead. The whole impression happens in one visit; border molding and the PVS impression are taken in a single seating (after border-molding compound is in place but before it's been re-trimmed).

items: Using the preliminary alginate impression, pour a diagnostic cast.

Place 2 sheets of soft baseplate wax over the teeth and tooth-supported edentulous areas. Cut small openings through the baseplate wax to allow fabrication of three tissue stops on the impression tray.
Place 1 sheet of soft baseplate wax over the distal extension areas.
Cover the wax spacer with Triad or acrylic resin.

Using a scalpel, trim the vestibular extension of the tray 2 mm short of the full depth of the vestibule. Add finger rests on top of the tray in the 1st molar regions. Anteriorly, place the tray handle so it doesn't interfere with the impression-taking procedure.
Cure the tray for 2 minutes; flip and cure the intaglio for 1 more minute.
Remove 2 mm of wax from the borders of the intaglio of the custom tray to allow space for the border molding compound.
**Check custom tray for extension.** Seat dry, verify borders are 2 mm short of functional sulcus depth. Adjust with acrylic bur if any borders are over-extended.

**Perform border molding** in the distal extension areas, and along the entire lingual vestibule for mandibular cases. Heat green stick over alcohol torch, paint onto borders, seat in mouth, have patient perform functional movements (lip protrusion/retraction, tongue movements). Repeat per segment.

**Reduce thickness of the border molding** to 0.5 mm with a sharp blade. (This leaves room for the impression material to seal at the borders without bulging the borders out further than function would.)
**Remove baseplate wax** from inside the tray.

**Place a relief hole** in the maxillary tray (NOT in the mandibular tray) — typically in the center of the palate. Allows excess impression material to escape during seating instead of building back-pressure that displaces tissue.

**Apply PVS adhesive** and let it dry for ten minutes. (standard: use PVS-specific adhesive, NOT alginate spray. Dry time matters — too short and the adhesive won't bond; impression material peels off the tray during removal.)

**Take PVS impression after border molding.** Use light body on the teeth (syringed onto rest seats, guide planes, and abutment surfaces for fine detail). Load the remaining tray with medium or heavy body impression material. Seat with light pressure, hold until set (typically 5 minutes).
**Box the impression** (rim it with utility wax + a thin sheet of baseplate wax around the borders, creating a vessel for the pour).
**Pour the impression with Type III stone** (Coe-Cal or Microstone). Allow to set fully.

**After 24–48 hours, soak the cast in warm water to soften the border molding compound** — this is critical. The green stick is brittle when cold; warm water makes it pliable so you can release the cast from the impression without breaking the cast borders.
**Gently separate the impression from the cast.** Pull straight off along the path of insertion of the tray, not at an angle.
**Trim the cast**, preserving the vestibule depth (this is critical for denture base extension at processing). Trim only the bulk material — never trim into the functional border.

blocks.title: Why this method over altered cast
blocks.body: Fewer clinical and laboratory steps → fewer chances for procedural errors. The single-step technique uses controlled relief (wax spacer thickness) + viscose impression material (medium/heavy body) to take a functional impression of the distal extension areas — close to what altered cast achieves, with less complexity. Reserve altered cast technique for cases where the distal extension tissue is unusually compressible or where the framework rotation needs more precise tissue compensation.

blocks.text: For Kennedy I and II distal-extension cases where you want the tissue under the distal extensions captured under functional load (not at rest). Two impressions: an anatomic impression for framework fabrication, then a functional impression of the distal extension areas with the framework as the carrier.

Master cast was poured from the FIRST impression (anatomic, with custom tray, just like step 5). Framework was fabricated from that master cast. Framework comes back to clinic for try-in. Altered cast impression happens AT the framework try-in appointment.
Step by step (12 steps per Kim)

items: **Intraorally, verify seating of the RPD framework** and its fit with the disclosing wax.

**Check for a discrepancy** of the framework's relation with the edentulous area(s) on the master cast and intraorally. Patient may have flabby compressible tissue in the distal extension(s) and a significant discrepancy in seating the tissue stops between the framework on the master cast vs intraorally.
**Vaseline the edentulous area(s) on the master cast** and add a layer of soft baseplate wax as a spacer for the impression material.
**Heat up the edentulous side(s) of the framework** and fully seat it on the cast.
**Remove wax from the top of the lattice.**
**Add a layer of Triad on top of the lattice area**, 2 mm short of the vestibule to allow space for border molding compound.
**After curing Triad custom tray, remove baseplate wax** from underneath. (If Triad is not secure, add a thin layer of Pattern resin to secure the custom tray to the framework.)
**Border mold distal extension area(s)** with molding compound (green stick).
**Reduce border molding compound** to 0.5 mm.
**Apply PVS adhesive** on the intaglio surface of the tray and wait ten minutes to dry.

**Take impression of the distal extension area(s)** with light or medium body PVS, while border molding. **CRITICAL:** make sure the rest seats are fully seated and you press on the rest seats ONLY. **Do NOT press on the distal extension area(s) of the metal framework** — pressing there compresses the soft tissue under load and defeats the entire purpose of the altered cast.

**Send the impression with the framework to the dental laboratory.** The lab will section the master cast at the distal extension regions, make retentive undercuts, bead + box + pour new distal extension segments from your altered impression, and use the corrected master cast to process the RPD.

blocks.title: Why the "press only on rest seats" rule matters
blocks.body: The point of altered cast is to capture the distal extension tissue in the SAME compressed state it will be in when the patient bites down. The natural-tooth rests are the load-bearing anchors during function; the framework rotates around them as the saddle settles into the soft tissue. If you press on the saddle areas during impression, you compress the tissue further than it would be compressed in function — the resulting denture base sits too deep, and after fabrication you have a partial that rocks toward the saddle in occlusion. Press the REST SEATS firmly. The saddle finds its own position.

chapters.title: The Preliminary Partial Denture Design Form

blocks.text: The Lab 4 form is completed before the tooth-preparation appointment. It serves two purposes — a personal checklist so no component is forgotten, and a faculty sign-off gate before any tooth modification begins.
Seven elements — every one must be addressed

items: **Guide planes and their references.** Mark each abutment that hosts a guide plane and note the proximal surface (mesial / distal) and the reference for the path of insertion.

**Rest seats and reduction amounts**, including indirect retainers. Where does each rest go? How much occlusal reduction is needed? Indirect retainers (typically cingulum rests on canines for Kennedy I/II distal extensions) get marked too.
**Undercuts that need to be created** — location and depth. Mark abutments where the natural undercut isn't usable and you'll need to add composite or plan a survey crown.

**Heights of contour that need to be adjusted**, especially for suprabulge clasps (Akers) and reciprocal clasps. If the HOC is too high, the clasp tip can't reach the gingival 1/3 — adjustment lowers it.
**Types of clasps.** Per tooth, specify: Akers, Reverse Akers, RPI, T-bar, Y-bar, Embrasure, Ring, Combination, etc.
**Minor connectors.** The portions of the framework that connect each clasp assembly back to the major connector.
**Major connector.** Lingual bar, lingual plate, A-P strap, palatal plate, anterior-posterior strap, etc.
**Acrylic portions** of the denture (the saddle bases) — drawn / outlined in red.
**Undercuts** — exact location marked in red, with the depth noted.
**HOC circles** on each abutment — circles drawn in red around the surveyed HOC.
**Tissue stop** locations — distal extensions get distal tissue stops, marked in red on the framework drawing.

blocks.title: Use it at the clinical appointment too
blocks.body: Bring the design form to the prep appointment. As you complete each modification chairside, check it off on the form. This catches the "wait, did I do guide plane on #20?" question before you discharge the patient.

blocks.text: Survey crowns are crowns fabricated to be fitted with an RPD when correction of acceptable tooth contours cannot be achieved with modification of the occlusal surfaces of the residual teeth. The prep has to make room for the surveyed features (rest seat, guide plane, undercut) AND the crown material itself. Plan 90 min for the prep appointment, separate from the RPD design appointments.

items: **Mounted diagnostic casts** (Denar 320, face-bow + Regisil bite reg).
**Surveyor** + analyzing rod + undercut gauges (for designing the crown on the diagnostic cast first).
**Wax-up materials:** inlay wax (blue), PKT instruments, Hanau torch, surveyor with wax knife (for surveying the wax-up).

**Prep burs for chairside:** coarse-grit chamfer diamond (4 mm tapered), fine-grit chamfer diamond (for finish), football diamond (for occlusal reduction including the rest seat depth), round-end taper diamond.
**Final impression:** light + medium body PVS (Aquasil or equivalent), or heavy body PVS in a stock or custom tray.
**Temporization:** Protemp or Integrity provisional material, temporary cement (Tempbond NE non-eugenol).
**Bite registration:** Regisil PB or fast-set PVS bite material.
**Lab Rx** form with full prescription details (see Step 6).

blocks.text: Pre-prep planning (in the lab, before the patient appointment)

items: Diagnostic casts mounted + surveyed (+ complete).
RPD framework designed on the diagnostic cast (Lab 4 Design Form complete).
**Duplicate the diagnostic cast** with an alginate impression of the cast + pour a second cast for practice prep + wax-up.
On the duplicate, practice the abutment preparation in wax (or with burs on the stone cast) — reduce the area where the survey crown will sit.
Wax up the survey crown to its **ideal contour** — with the planned rest seat, guide plane, undercut, and HOC.
Survey the waxed crown on the surveyor: verify positive rest seat, ideal HOC + axial undercut, ideal guide plane orientation.
Photograph or sketch the planned crown contours for the lab Rx.
**1a.** Local anesthesia per the planned prep tooth (typically 1 carpule 2% lidocaine 1:100,000 epi for posterior teeth; PSA / IA blocks for molars; infiltration for premolars + anteriors).
**1b.** Verify anesthesia depth: tooth feels nothing to firm explorer probing.
**1c.** Isolation: rubber dam where feasible (recommended for moisture control during prep); cotton roll + Dri-Angle alternative for posterior crowns.
**1d.** Confirm with patient: comfort check, hand-raise signal established.

**2a. Occlusal reduction:** use the football diamond to reduce occlusal surface. **2.5–3.0 mm in the area of the planned rest seat** (deeper than a standard crown's 1.5 mm — you need bulk for the rest seat to be carved in the crown material). 1.5 mm elsewhere on the occlusal surface.
**2b. Axial reduction:** use the chamfer diamond. 1.0 mm chamfer all around (the chamfer margin allows the lab to design the crown with adequate bulk + smooth marginal seal).
**2c. Guide plane region:** reduce the planned guide plane proximal surface so it's parallel to the RPD path of insertion. The crown will be built up flat in this region.
**2d. Undercut region:** over-reduce the planned undercut surface slightly so the lab can build the crown out with adequate bulk for the 0.01" undercut at the gingival 1/3.
**2e.** Verify reduction with a periodontal probe or the Brasseler reduction guide: 2.5–3.0 mm at the rest seat location, 1.5 mm elsewhere occlusally, 1.0 mm axially.
**2f.** Finalize margin with the fine-grit chamfer diamond.
**3a.** Single-cord retraction for the survey crown abutment (one #00 cord, 5 min retention).
**3b.** Mix + load: light body PVS in the syringe, medium or heavy body in the tray.
**3c.** Remove cord, syringe light body around the margin, then over the prep.
**3d.** Seat the tray with medium / heavy body. Hold until set (typically 5 min).
**3e.** Remove. Inspect: margin captured cleanly, no voids, no tears.

blocks.text: Step 4 — Opposing impression + bite registration (10 min)

items: **4a.** Take an alginate opposing impression (or PVS if a final crown opposing is needed).
**4b.** Take a bite registration in MI with Regisil PB or fast-set PVS bite material.
**4c.** Confirm the bite reg articulates cleanly with both casts.
**5a.** Make a quick provisional from the pre-prep matrix (alginate or PVS matrix of the diagnostic cast).
**5b. Critical:** the provisional must include the planned **rest seat** (so the patient can wear the existing RPD or planned interim partial while the survey crown is fabricated).
**5c.** Adjust occlusion to be in light contact (not high — the patient will wear this for 2–3 weeks).
**5d.** Cement with Tempbond NE (non-eugenol, won't interfere with final cementation adhesion).

blocks.text: The lab Rx for a survey crown is more detailed than a standard crown Rx because the crown has to be designed to integrate with the RPD framework. Include:

items: **Crown material:** PFM or full-cast metal (depending on esthetic location). default: PFM for anterior + premolar; full-cast metal for molars.
**Metal thickness:** 0.5–1.0 mm in the rest seat area.
**Rest seat:** location (M / D / cingulum), dimensions (1.5 mm deep, 2.5 mm wide, floor angled toward the long axis of the tooth).
**Guide plane:** proximal surface flat + parallel to the RPD path of insertion (provide the cast tripod marks to the lab so they can mount + survey their working cast in the same orientation).
**Undercut:** location (mid-B, MB, DB, ML, DL), depth (0.01" for cast clasp; 0.02" for wrought wire), in the gingival 1/3.
**HOC:** where it should fall relative to the gingival 1/3 (typically the junction of the middle 1/3 and the gingival 1/3).
**RPD path of insertion:** marked on the working cast with tripod dots.
**Enclosed:** final impression, opposing impression, bite reg, surveyed + tripod-marked diagnostic cast (as reference for the lab).

blocks.text: Step 7 — Verify wax-up before final firing

items: **7a.** Ask the lab to send back the **full-contour wax-up** of the survey crown before they apply porcelain (PFM) or invest (full-cast).
**7b.** Verify on the surveyor: tripod the lab's working cast (using the tripod dots you marked), then lower the analyzing rod.

**7c. Check:** positive rest seat (clear depression in the wax, with floor angled toward the long axis), ideal HOC at the planned location, 0.01" usable undercut at the gingival 1/3 on the planned surface, guide plane parallel to the path of insertion.
**7d.** If anything is wrong, mark on the wax-up with a pencil and send back for correction. Cheaper to re-wax than to refire / recast.
**7e.** Approve in writing on the lab Rx. Lab proceeds to final firing.

blocks.title: Verify the survey crown wax-up before final firing
blocks.body: Ask the laboratory to send back the full-contour wax-up of the survey crown for you to make changes if necessary. **Verify contours of the survey crown(s) using the surveyor when they're returned from the lab BEFORE cementation.** Evaluate for: POSITIVE rest seats, ideal height of contour (HOC) + axial undercuts, ideal guide plane(s).

blocks.title: Issues
blocks.body: **Reduction was too shallow at the rest seat:** the lab will report inadequate metal thickness. Re-prep at the next visit (deepen rest seat area by 0.5 mm). **Reduction was too deep:** over-prepped, no fix needed but margins may need to extend further apically for adequate retention. **Provisional fractures at the rest seat:** patient was biting hard. Re-make the provisional with more occlusal clearance + reinforce with wire mesh if needed. **Wax-up arrives without the rest seat:** lab missed it on the Rx. Send back; do not approve. **HOC is in the wrong location on the wax-up:** survey the wax-up on your surveyor, mark the desired HOC location in pencil on the cast, send back. **Final crown returns with no usable undercut:** the lab made the contour too straight. You can add composite to the cemented crown at the gingival 1/3 to create the undercut chairside (etch the porcelain or roughen the metal, bond, composite). Discuss with faculty before adjusting.

chapters.title: How to write the lab Rx

blocks.text: The lab Rx is your communication tool with the dental laboratory technician. Aim for clear and effective communication. ALL the information must be included. Draw the design of the RPD framework on the tooth chart. Sloppy Rxs come back as sloppy frameworks.

items: **General Information:** student/doctor name, patient name.
**Supplements** (what's enclosed in the case pan): Master Cast, Mounted on Denar 320, Opposing cast, Design cast.
**For Department Use Only:** laboratory destination (e.g. Ottawa UG/DSP), date ordered, date due back, order # / case #.
**Procedure Code:** D5213 for mandibular cast partial; D5214 for maxillary.
**Site:** UA (upper arch) or LA (lower arch).
**Instructions box** — the prescription itself (see below).
**Tooth chart** at the bottom — draw the framework design.
**Instructor name + signature line.**
**Opening:** "Please fabricate Co-Cr metal framework for [maxillary / mandibular] RPD."
**Major connector line:** "Major connector: [type] with 0.5mm beading on the tissue side."

**Per-abutment lines** (one per abutment): "[tooth#]: [rest location] rest, [proximal plate location] proximal plate, [clasp type] engaging 0.01" [undercut location] undercut, [reciprocal] reciprocal clasp."
**Indirect retainer line** if applicable: "[tooth#]: cingulum rest."
**Saddle lines:** "[teeth]: Open Lattice" (or Mesh, Tube Tooth, Facing). Add "with distal tissue stop" for distal extensions.
**Closing:** "Please return for try-in. Thank you."

blocks.title: Sample lab Rx (mandibular)
blocks.body:
Please fabricate Co-Cr metal framework for mandibular RPD.

**Major connector:** Lingual Bar (4mm height, 4mm clearance from gingival margin), 0.5mm beading on tissue surface.
**Path of insertion:** per surveyed cast.
**Undercuts to engage:** marked in red on the cast.

**Direct retainers**
#18 — Mesial proximal plate · Mesial rest · Akers clasp engaging 0.01" mid-buccal undercut · Lingual reciprocal clasp.
#20 — Distal proximal plate · Distal rest · Akers clasp engaging 0.01" mid-buccal undercut · Lingual reciprocal clasp.
#28 — Distal proximal plate · Distal rest · Akers clasp engaging 0.01" mid-buccal undercut · Lingual reciprocal clasp.
#31 — Mesial proximal plate · Mesial rest · Akers clasp engaging 0.01" mid-buccal undercut · Lingual reciprocal clasp.

**Saddle / base design**
#19 — Open Lattice.
#29, #30 — Open Lattice.

**Tooth selection (deferred to framework try-in)**
Tooth shade — TBD chairside.
Anterior mold — TBD (Trubyte Classic).
Posterior mold — TBD (Trubyte Classic posterior, e.g. F30 10°).
Gingival shade — TBD (typically L199-OR or 50% OR + 50% DK mix).

**Enclosed**
Master cast (mounted on Denar 320 via facebow transfer; tripod marks + undercuts marked in red), opposing cast, Regisil PVS bite registration recorded at MI.

Please return for framework try-in. Thank you.

items: **Master cast:** tripod marks, exact points of undercut in RED, HOC of abutment teeth marked.
**Design cast:** full framework design drawn. Metal components in BLUE. Tissue stops, wrought wire, and acrylic base extensions in RED.

blocks.text: Pressure-indicating paste (PIP) reveals where the intaglio of an RPD is pressing too hard on the tissue. You paint it on, seat the RPD, and the areas of contact show as displaced paste. Mizzy spray keeps the PIP from migrating around the patient's mouth, lips, and cheeks during the procedure. Plan ~30 min including iterations.

items: **PIP paste** (pressure-indicating paste, white) + applicator brush (soft, short bristles).
**Mizzy spray** (silicone-based film; standard).
**Acrylic adjustment burs** (carbide, cross-cut, fine, on slow-speed handpiece).
**Polishing wheels** (rag wheel + pumice slurry; high-shine compound + clean rag wheel).
**Patient bib** + cheek retractors (optional, useful for posterior teeth visualization).
**Mouth mirror** + good intraoral lighting.
**Disposable cup of water** for rinsing the RPD between iterations.
**Gauze** for drying the RPD intaglio between iterations.
RPD is fresh from delivery prep (intaglio clean of any lab polish, dry).
Patient seated, semi-upright. Bib clipped. Cheek retractors at hand if posterior tissue stops are involved.
PIP brush bristles tested — should sweep paste in clean parallel strokes (replace brush if frayed).
Mizzy spray verified working — shake well, test-spray into the trash to confirm aerosol.
Lab handpiece + acrylic burs ready on the bracket table.
Polishing setup ready at the chairside lathe.

blocks.text: Step 1 — Confirm RPD is clean + dry (1 min)

items: **1a.** Wipe the intaglio with gauze — remove any saliva, debris, or lab polish residue.
**1b.** Confirm dry to the touch — wet intaglio causes PIP to streak.
**1c.** Inspect for visible acrylic flash or sharp edges — smooth chairside with the acrylic bur before applying PIP (these would show as false positives).

blocks.text: Step 2 — Apply PIP in one direction (2 min)

items: **2a.** Load the brush with a small amount of PIP paste (~2×2 mm bead on the bristles).
**2b.** Brush onto the intaglio in **parallel strokes, all in the same direction** — start at one end of the partial and sweep toward the other.
**2c.** Coat the entire intaglio with a thin, uniform layer. Too thick = paste won't read pressure. Too thin = the pattern is invisible.
**2d.** Keep the brush strokes *parallel* — the directional brush pattern is what you'll read for pressure spots after seating.
**3a.** Hold the Mizzy can ~6 in (15 cm) from the RPD intaglio.
**3b.** Light, even spray over the entire PIP-coated surface.
**3c.** Mizzy forms a silicone film that limits PIP migration in the patient's mouth. Without it, the PIP smears off the partial onto cheeks + lips, and you can't read the pattern.
**3d.** Let dry to a slight tackiness (~10–15 sec).

blocks.text: Step 4 — Seat the RPD in the patient (5 min)

items: **4a.** Identify the path of insertion (from the design + lab Rx).
**4b.** Carefully seat the RPD along that path. **Do not** press on the saddle areas (for distal extensions) or rest seats (initially) — you're letting it find its seated position naturally.
**4c.** Once fully seated, have the patient **lightly close into MI** (light occlusal load only; you're testing tissue contact, not function).
**4d.** Hold for **1–2 minutes**. The PIP displaces under pressure during this time.
**4e.** Have the patient open. Carefully remove the RPD — lift along the path of removal so the intaglio comes out clean without smearing.
**5a.** Place the RPD intaglio-up on the bracket table under good light.
**5b. Areas where the brush-direction pattern is intact** = no contact (or appropriate light contact). These are fine.
**5c. Areas where the paste has been wiped clean back to the acrylic** = pressure points. These need adjustment.

**5d. Areas of partial displacement (smudged, half-erased pattern)** = moderate contact. Adjust if the patient reports discomfort; otherwise leave (mild even contact is desired for distal extensions).
**5e.** Take a photo of the intaglio with phone for the chart, especially if you're going to do multiple iterations.

blocks.text: Step 6 — Adjust pressure points (5 min per iteration)

items: **6a.** Mark the pressure spots with a pencil on the intaglio (cross out the wiped areas so you don't lose them once you start adjusting).

**6b.** Use the acrylic bur at *low speed* on slow-speed handpiece. Remove a **thin** layer of acrylic from each pressure spot. **Small amounts at a time** — you can remove more later, you can't put it back.
**6c.** Smooth each adjustment with a finer bur, then with a pumice wheel.
**6d.** **Do not** adjust the metal framework — PIP shows tissue pressure, which is an acrylic issue. If you suspect framework contact, use disclosing wax instead (see callout).
**6e.** Rinse the RPD intaglio. Dry with gauze.
**6f.** Re-apply PIP + Mizzy (Steps 2–3). Re-seat in the patient (Step 4). Re-interpret (Step 5).
**6g.** Repeat until **no significant pressure spots remain** (small light contact areas are acceptable).
**7a.** Once pressure spots are resolved, polish all adjusted areas with pumice slurry on a rag wheel.
**7b.** High-shine the cameo surface with high-shine compound.
**7c. Do not high-shine the intaglio** — the matte texture aids retention via capillary action with saliva.
**7d.** Disinfect the RPD per the protocol. Patient may keep wearing it.

blocks.title: PIP for tissue, disclosing wax for metal
blocks.body: **PIP** reads soft-tissue contact under acrylic. **Disclosing wax** reads metal-on-tooth contact — thin layer of green wax painted on the framework's tissue surface, seated; metal contact wipes the wax through to the metal. Use disclosing wax to verify framework seating on natural teeth (rest seats, guide planes); use PIP to verify acrylic-base seating on tissue.

blocks.title: Issues
blocks.body: **PIP smears around the patient's mouth and onto lips/cheeks:** you skipped the Mizzy spray or sprayed too lightly. Wipe clean, restart with proper Mizzy application. **PIP shows no clear pattern (uniform pink):** too thick. Re-apply thinner. **PIP shows no pattern (mostly bare acrylic):** too thin. Re-apply thicker. **Every iteration shows the same pressure spot in the same location:** deeper issue. Could be a sharp internal feature in the acrylic, or a tissue prominence not captured at impression. Investigate intraorally; if a torus or ridge undercut is the cause, consider relief area in the acrylic. **Patient reports pain but PIP shows nothing:** the issue may be a sharp acrylic edge on the periphery (not the intaglio). Run a thumb pad around all borders + look for sharp acrylic at the labial / buccal flange edge. Smooth with the acrylic bur. **Removed too much acrylic, RPD now loose:** reline the affected area chairside with chairside reline material (e.g., Coe-Soft or hard chairside reline acrylic), or send to the lab for hard reline. Easier to over-relieve and reline than to deliver an uncomfortable partial. **Excessive adjustments needed (>5 iterations):** the impression or framework fit is off. Document, consult faculty — may need to re-impress or refabricate.

blocks.text: These are the locations where over-extended acrylic or excessive denture base thickness most commonly causes patient discomfort at delivery and follow-up. Check these specifically with PIP — they're the highest-yield places to look.

items: **Buccal posterior border areas parallel to the tuberosities.**
**Hamular notch** (bilaterally).
**Over-extension of the acrylic** in the areas of the temporal root of the zygomatic arch.
**Rugae** (the firm ridges of the anterior palate — sensitive to compression).
**Mid-palatal suture** or **torus palatinus** if present.
**Lingual part of the mandibular ridge**, especially in the premolar area.
**Mylohyoid ridge** (the ridge on the lingual mandible at the level of the mylohyoid attachment).
**Over-extended acrylic at the buccal shelf** (the broad cortical-bone area lateral to the alveolar ridge).
**Retromylohyoid space** (just distal to the mylohyoid ridge).
**Retromolar pad** — distal soft-tissue cushion behind the last molar. Should be covered partially but not over-extended.
**Disto-buccal border of the acrylic** in the area of the ascending ramus and the external oblique ridge.
**Tori (exostoses) and bony irregularities** — uneven bone healing after extractions, bony spicules.

blocks.title: Why these specific locations
blocks.body: **Maxilla**: the rugae and mid-palatal suture have firm cortical bone close to the surface, with little soft-tissue cushion — they don't tolerate compression. The hamular notch and tuberosities are common sites where the lab over-extends the acrylic past the functional sulcus. **Mandible**: the lingual side of the premolar area sits over the mylohyoid muscle attachment — pressure here causes sublingual discomfort during tongue movements. The buccal shelf and retromolar pad are over-extension targets for distal-extension partials, where the lab tries to maximize support and ends up impinging on movable mucosa.

blocks.text: Adjusting RPD occlusion isn't the same as adjusting natural-tooth occlusion. The denture teeth are processed in a lab against a mounted master cast; the natural teeth in the patient's mouth occlude as the patient's stomatognathic system has adapted them to over years. The goal at delivery is to make the denture teeth join that system without disrupting it. Plan ~30 min for occlusal adjustment (more if bilateral).

items: **Articulating paper:** AccuFilm II (double-sided red/red, thin film) is preferred for precision. Surgident full-arch (blue/red, paper) as backup.
**Articulating paper holder** (Miller forceps or equivalent).
**Acrylic adjustment bur** (carbide, cross-cut, on slow-speed handpiece).
**Football diamond bur** for adjusting metal (only if absolutely necessary — see callout below).
**Polishing wheels:** pumice slurry on rag wheel, then high-shine compound on a clean rag wheel.
**Mouth mirror** + cheek retractors.
**Disclosing wax** (thin green wax sheet) for checking metal framework contact, in case of suspected framework interference.
**Bur block** with appropriate burs at the bracket table.
**Patient bib**, good lighting.
RPD pressure spots already resolved (PIP/Mizzy complete) — you don't want PIP issues to confound occlusal interpretation.
Patient seated semi-upright, comfortable.
Verify rest seats engage cleanly on the natural teeth (visual check before any closing).
Confirm with patient: comfort, signal established.

blocks.text: Step 1 — Baseline: natural-tooth occlusion without RPDs (5 min)

items: **1a.** Remove the RPD(s) from the patient's mouth.
**1b.** Place articulating paper between the remaining natural teeth.
**1c.** Have the patient bite into MI (Tap-Tap-Tap, no grinding).
**1d.** Remove paper. Identify the natural-tooth contact pattern (which teeth contact, how heavily, in what distribution).
**1e.** **This is your baseline.** You will preserve this pattern after the RPDs are inserted.
**1f.** Photograph for the chart if helpful.

blocks.text: Step 2 — Seat the first RPD + verify natural contacts (5 min)

items: **2a.** Seat the first RPD along the path of insertion.
**2b.** Verify the metal framework seats fully on the natural teeth: rests engaged, guide planes flush, clasps not flexing in an unusual position.
**2c.** Place articulating paper between the natural teeth (same teeth as Step 1).
**2d.** Patient bites into MI.
**2e.** Remove paper. **The natural teeth should contact the same way they did without the RPD.**
**2f. If natural-tooth contacts changed** (lighter, missing, or shifted): the RPD is interfering somewhere. **Proceed to Step 3.**
**2g. If natural-tooth contacts match the baseline:** good. Proceed to Step 4 (test the second RPD if bilateral, or verify excursives).

blocks.text: Step 3 — Locate + adjust interferences (5–15 min per iteration)

items: **3a.** Place articulating paper between **all** teeth (natural + denture).
**3b.** Patient bites into MI.
**3c.** Remove. Inspect ALL teeth for marks. **Heavy mark on a denture tooth + light or absent mark on a natural tooth** = the denture tooth is hitting first and propping the bite.

**3d. Verify the framework is not the cause:** use disclosing wax on the framework's tissue surface (proximal plate, rest seat). Re-seat. If the wax wipes through to bare metal under the rest, the framework is interfering, not the denture teeth — *do not grind the denture teeth; document and refer back to the lab for framework adjustment.*
**3e.** If framework is fine and a denture tooth has the heavy mark: **gradually reduce** the denture tooth with the acrylic bur. Small amount (0.1–0.2 mm) at a time.

**3f. Adjust the non-centric-holding cusps first.** BULL applies: **B**uccal of **U**ppers + **L**ingual of **L**owers are non-centric-holding. Avoid reducing the maxillary lingual cusps + mandibular buccal cusps (centric-holding) — reducing them collapses the bite.
**3g.** Re-check with articulating paper. Iterate Steps 3a–3f until natural-tooth contacts match the baseline + denture teeth contact lightly + evenly.

blocks.text: Step 4 — Test the second RPD (if bilateral case) (5–10 min)

items: **4a.** With the first RPD in place + occlusion verified, seat the second RPD.
**4b.** Repeat Steps 2 + 3 with both RPDs in.

**4c. The second RPD must accommodate the first.** The second RPD's denture teeth must contact lightly with the first RPD's denture teeth (and any natural antagonists) in MI without disrupting the now-verified first RPD's occlusion.
**4d.** Iterate adjustment of the second RPD's denture teeth until both partials + natural teeth all contact simultaneously in MI.
**5a.** Once MI is correct, place articulating paper between teeth.

**5b. Right lateral excursion:** patient slides jaw to the right while keeping teeth touching. Inspect marks afterward. There should be working-side guidance on natural canines / posterior teeth without working-side or non-working-side interferences from the denture teeth.
**5c. Left lateral excursion:** same.
**5d. Protrusive excursion:** patient slides jaw forward. Anterior teeth (natural or denture) should disclude the posteriors. No posterior denture-tooth interferences.

**5e.** Adjust per BULL rule if interferences found: **B**uccal of **U**ppers + **L**ingual of **L**owers are the non-centric-holding cusp inclines — adjust those to clear excursive interferences. Don't touch centric-holding cusps.
**6a.** Once occlusion is correct in MI + excursions, polish all adjusted denture-tooth surfaces.
**6b.** Pumice slurry on rag wheel → high-shine compound on clean rag wheel.
**6c.** Verify smooth occlusal surfaces with explorer — no roughness, no overhangs.
**6d.** Re-seat the RPD(s) one final time, confirm with the patient that everything feels even.
**6e.** Document the adjustment + photo of final contact pattern.

blocks.title: Why "denture teeth, not framework"
blocks.body: The framework's rest seats were verified at framework try-in (Appointment 5). If the framework now interferes in occlusion, it's almost always because acrylic over the framework added bulk — the acrylic, not the metal, is the culprit. Adjust the acrylic. If you suspect framework interference (e.g. a rest seat that wasn't deep enough on the abutment), use disclosing wax on the framework's tissue surface to confirm — and if confirmed, send back to lab. **Don't grind metal at delivery.**

blocks.title: BULL rule
blocks.body: **B**uccal of **U**ppers + **L**ingual of **L**owers are the non-centric-holding cusps. These are the safe cusps to adjust for excursive interferences. The centric-holding cusps (maxillary lingual + mandibular buccal) are off-limits for adjustment — reducing them collapses VDO.

blocks.title: Issues
blocks.body: **Patient bites into the RPD before the natural teeth contact:** denture teeth are too tall. Adjust until natural + denture contact simultaneously. **Natural teeth contact but RPD denture teeth don't reach:** denture teeth are too short, or the RPD isn't fully seated. Verify seating; if seated, the lab built the denture teeth short — may need to add material at the lab. **One RPD interferes with the other:** common for bilateral cases. Adjust the second RPD's denture teeth (since the first was adjusted to match natural occlusion first). **Excursive interferences from the canines on the RPD:** if canine guidance is provided by an RPD denture tooth, adjust the disclusive surface (lingual incline of maxillary canine, buccal incline of mandibular canine) — BULL rule. **Patient says it feels off but you can't find anything:** get the patient to localize. Sometimes the issue is on the opposite side (cross-arch interference). Mark teeth on both sides; let the patient close in MI and trace where their tongue feels resistance. **Adjustment marks confirm framework metal contact:** stop. Disclosing wax check; if confirmed, send the RPD back to the lab for rest-seat depth correction. Never grind a Co-Cr framework chairside.

blocks.text: What you tell the patient before they walk out with their new partial. Cover all of these or you'll get a phone call in 48 hours.

items: "For the first 24–48 hours, wear it as much as you can tolerate, including overnight. This helps your tissues adapt and identifies any sore spots quickly so we can adjust them at your follow-up."

"After the first follow-up, switch to wearing it during the day only. **Take it out at night** to give your gum tissue and the underlying bone a chance to rest. Sleeping in a partial can cause inflammation and bone loss long-term."
"If you take it out during the day to eat something sticky or for a quick break, that's fine — just always rinse it and your mouth before putting it back in."
"Brush the partial daily with a soft toothbrush. You can use regular toothpaste, but a special denture cleaner works better."

"Pay extra attention to your natural teeth where the clasps sit — those are the places where plaque collects fastest and where you can get cavities or gum disease. Brush around the clasps thoroughly and floss your teeth as usual."
"Soak the partial in a denture cleanser (like Polident or equivalent) overnight while it's out, then rinse before putting it back in."
