Retake the impression at the next clinical visit. **Stone chips at the margin during trimming:** die may have been undercured (poured too soon or wrong W:P). Re-pour from impression.

items: Poor emergence profile.
Overcontoured final restoration.
Bulky restoration that promotes plaque accumulation and gingival inflammation.

blocks.text: Trimming **away from** the finish line to expose it for the lab — leaves a small ditch at the margin that the wax-up will fill cleanly. **Distinct from over-trimming**, which destroys the finish line itself.

chapters.verdict: The lab step that determines emergence profile, crown contour, and gingival response to the final restoration. Over-trim and you get bulky overcontoured crowns that promote plaque and inflammation.

chapters.title: Diagnostic wax-up + lost-wax casting

items: **Mounted master cast** with trimmed dies (+ complete).
**Inlay wax** (blue or green; stocks Yeti or GC inlay wax) + Hanau / Bunsen torch.
**PKT instruments** (PKT 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 — pear / cone / round shapes for adding + carving wax).
**Le Cron carver** for fine detail.
**#7 wax spatula** for adding wax.
**Die spacer / die hardener** (Tru-Fit or equivalent) + brush + dappen dish.
**Articulator** (mounted) with diagnostic casts ready.
**Sprue wax** (8-gauge or 10-gauge depending on the unit size) + sprue base / crucible former.
**Investment material:** phosphate-bonded investment for PFM / high-temp alloys; gypsum-bonded for low-temp gold.
**Casting ring** + liner (asbestos-free ceramic).
**Burnout furnace** (programmable; common: Whip Mix Pro-Press furnace).
**Centrifuge caster** + crucible + alloy (matched to the casting program).
**Divestment tools:** shot blaster, abrasive wheels, separating discs.
**Loupes** (2.5× minimum) for detailed wax-up work.
Working cast mounted on articulator (complete).
Dies trimmed per protocol (complete) — finish lines clean, root form smooth, dies independently removable.
Die spacer applied to the die: 1 coat over the prep area, stopping 0.5 mm short of the finish line. Let dry per spec (typically 5–10 min).
Lab handpiece + bur block at the bench.
Articulator settings verified: condylar inclination per protrusive record (default 30°), centric latch verified.

blocks.text: Step 1 — Full-contour wax-up (60–90 min for single unit)

items: **1a.** Apply die spacer (already done in setup) to allow cement film thickness (25 µm water-based, 50 µm resin-based).

**1b. Establish proximal contacts first.** Wax the mesial + distal contacts with the adjacent teeth. Contacts should be in the cervical 1/3 of the contact area, broad B-L, with embrasures preserved B + L + occlusal + cervical.
**1c. Add cervical contour.** Develop the emergence profile from the die's root form margin to the crown. Smooth, slightly bulging, not flat or undercut. Mimics natural emergence.

**1d. Add cusp tips.** Set cusp tip locations referenced to the antagonist cusps on the opposing cast. For posterior crowns: central groove of the mandibular = crest of ridge; maxillary lingual cusps over mandibular crest (cheek-bite rule).
**1e. Develop functional cusps** with proper height for the patient's occlusion.
**1f. Develop marginal ridges** at consistent height with the adjacent teeth's marginal ridges.
**1g. Add occlusal anatomy:** central groove, supplemental grooves, triangular ridges, fossae.
**1h. Verify centric contacts** on the articulator. Close into centric, articulating paper marks should be on the planned centric-holding cusps (max lingual, mand buccal) and central fossae.
**1i. Verify excursions.** Right lateral, left lateral, protrusive — no interferences.
**1j.** Refine surface texture: stipple facial surfaces with a toothbrush, develop developmental grooves, blend everything into a natural-looking crown.

blocks.text: Step 2 — Wax cut-back for porcelain space (PFM only) (30 min)

items: **2a.** Identify the regions that will be porcelain: facial + occlusal (for PFM, typically the entire visible surface plus 1–2 mm of occlusal table).
**2b.** Cut back the wax to leave a metal coping of **≥0.2–0.3 mm thickness** in the porcelain regions.
**2c.** Maintain a metal collar at the cervical (0.5–1.0 mm wide) for marginal seal — porcelain doesn't bond as cleanly at the margin.
**2d.** Ensure 0.8–2.0 mm of porcelain space everywhere it will be applied.
**2e.** Smooth all cut-back surfaces to allow uniform porcelain application.
**2f.** Verify metal coping thickness with calipers — the lab will measure during casting; under-thickness coping will distort or perforate during firing.
**3a.** Attach 8-gauge or 10-gauge sprue wax to the highest point of the wax pattern (typically the occlusal surface or the non-visible cervical area).
**3b.** Position sprue away from margins, contacts, and esthetic surfaces.
**3c.** Attach sprue base / crucible former to the casting ring.
**3d.** Position the wax pattern in the center of the ring (not too close to the walls or top; 5–10 mm clearance all around).
**3e.** Mix investment per manufacturer spec (specific W:P ratio + working time).
**3f.** Vacuum-mix to reduce porosity.
**3g.** Pour investment slowly into the ring, vibrating to release trapped air.
**3h.** Let set per spec (typically 1 hour).
**4a.** Place the ring (sprue side down) in the programmable burnout furnace.
**4b. Run the burnout program** (typical schedule: ramp to 200°C / hold 1 hour; ramp to 700°C / hold 1 hour; ramp to 900°C / hold 30 min). Programmable furnaces run automatically.
**4c.** The wax burns out cleanly, leaving a hollow mold cavity in the shape of the original wax pattern.
**4d.** When the program completes, the ring is at casting temperature (typically 700°C for the alloy being used).
**4e.** **Do NOT cool the ring before casting** — the mold needs to be hot for the molten alloy to flow into all the cavity detail.
**5a.** Wind the centrifuge spring (3 full turns for typical alloys; check the unit's spec).
**5b.** Place the hot casting ring in the centrifuge holder.
**5c.** Place the alloy ingot(s) in the crucible. Use the appropriate alloy mass for the unit size (typically 2× the wax pattern weight).
**5d.** Apply torch flame to the alloy in the crucible until it reaches the "molten and shiny + slight orange skin" appearance — the casting moment.
**5e.** Release the centrifuge. The spring rotates the arm at high speed, forcing the molten alloy from the crucible into the mold.
**5f.** Let the centrifuge complete its rotation. Stop the arm manually.
**5g.** Quench the ring in water (gypsum-bonded investment) or air-cool (phosphate-bonded). Quenching liberates the casting from the investment cleanly.
**6a.** Break away the investment with the abrasive wheel or shot blaster.
**6b.** Reveal the cast metal pattern with the sprue still attached.
**6c.** Cut off the sprue with a separating disc (don't grind into the crown material).
**6d.** Inspect the casting: no incomplete margins, no porosity, no shrinkage voids.
**6e.** Try the casting on the original die. Should seat fully without binding. If margins are short or rocking, the casting failed — remake.
**6f.** Finish surfaces with finishing burs / arbor bands. Polish margins for the framework try-in.

blocks.text: **High-noble** ≥40 wt% Au, ≥60 wt% noble. **Noble** ≥25 wt% noble. **Base** <25 wt% noble. Base alloys (Co, Ni, Cr) have superior mechanical properties and lower cost but: higher corrosion in acidic environments, more difficult finishing / polishing, allergy risk, and more difficult casting with marginal fit. High-noble / noble are easier to cast accurately, easier to finish, less allergenic, but more expensive.

blocks.title: Faculty checkpoints
blocks.body: **Inlay alloy?** High-noble (good ductility for burnishing margins, biocompatible, corrosion-resistant). **Long-span FPD alloy?** Base metal alloy (highest yield strength + elastic modulus for rigidity). **Grain refining elements (Ir, Ru, Rh, Re)?** Improve tensile strength + elongation ~30%; less effect on hardness and yield. **Base alloy disadvantages?** Corrosion, allergy, casting difficulty, finishing difficulty.

blocks.title: Issues
blocks.body: **Wax-up is too bulky:** over-contoured = bulky crown = plaque trap. Use calipers to check thickness; should match adjacent teeth. **Wax-up has open contacts:** add inlay wax to the contact areas; verify floss-tug at the same firmness as the contralateral side. **Cut-back leaves coping <0.2 mm:** coping will distort during porcelain firing. Re-wax to add bulk or remake. **Sprue attached to a margin or visible surface:** grind off after casting will leave a visible defect. Position sprue away from margins, contacts, esthetic surfaces. **Investment has air voids near the wax pattern:** visible as bumps on the casting. Re-invest with better vacuum mixing + vibration. **Casting has incomplete margins:** alloy didn't reach all areas of the mold cavity. Causes: ring cooled before casting, sprue too thin (alloy froze before filling), insufficient centrifuge force. Re-cast. **Casting has porosity:** mold was contaminated (residual wax), alloy was overheated (gas absorption), or centrifuge had air pockets. Re-cast. **Casting fits the die but margins are open clinically:** impression was inaccurate. Re-impress at next clinical visit. **Casting won't seat on the die:** investment expansion didn't match the alloy shrinkage. Acid-etch the intaglio briefly + try again; if persistent, remake with adjusted W:P ratio.

chapters.verdict: The wax-up establishes contour, contacts, and occlusion ideal for the lab to mill or cast against. Lost-wax casting then converts that wax pattern to alloy via investment, wax elimination, and centrifugal casting. Lab session 2–3 hours for a single-unit wax-up; 4–6 hours for an FPD framework wax-up + casting.

chapters.title: Porcelain application, characterization, bisque bake

items: **Tried-in metal framework** (complete, faculty-approved fit).
**Sandblaster** with 50 µm aluminum oxide (Al2O3).
**Porcelain kit** matched to the alloy CTE (Ivoclar IPS InLine for noble alloys, Vita VMK for various alloys): opaque + dentin + enamel + modifiers + correction porcelain.
**Distilled water** or porcelain mixing liquid + dappen dish + porcelain brushes (medium + fine).
**Vibrator** for compacting porcelain particles + reducing porosity.
**Gauze / tissue** for blotting moisture from condensed porcelain.
**Porcelain furnace** (Programat or Multimat) calibrated to the porcelain kit's firing schedule.
**Firing pin** + firing tray for placing the unit in the furnace.
**Stains + glaze** kit for characterization + final firing.
**Polishing kit:** diamond burs (fine), rubber points (blue + pink + gray) for porcelain.
**Mounted working cast** for evaluating contacts + occlusion.
Framework try-in complete + faculty-approved. Margins closed, no rocking, clearances verified.
Framework cleaned of all temporary cement, oils, debris.
Sandblaster operational; aluminum oxide media topped up.
Porcelain furnace warmed to standby temp.
Working cast ready for porcelain build-up + evaluation.
**1a.** Place the framework in the sandblaster chamber.
**1b.** Sandblast at 2–4 bar with 50 µm Al2O3 until the metal surface has a uniform matte gray appearance.
**1c. Goal:** uniform roughness for micromechanical retention + increased wettability for porcelain adhesion.
**1d.** Steam-clean to remove residual particles. Dry.
**2a.** Place the framework on a firing pin in the furnace.
**2b.** Run the **degas / oxidation cycle** per the porcelain manufacturer (typically 980°C in air, 1–2 min hold).
**2c.** Tin (Sn), indium (In), and gallium (Ga) in the alloy migrate to the surface to form a thin oxide layer.
**2d.** This oxide layer creates the chemical bond between metal + porcelain.
**2e.** Inspect the framework after oxidation: should have a uniform dark gray-blue oxide layer with no greasy or splotchy areas. If contaminated, sandblast + re-oxidize.
**3a.** Mix opaque porcelain with distilled water on the porcelain palette to a thick paste consistency.
**3b.** Apply a thin uniform layer to the entire metal surface that will be covered with porcelain.
**3c. Critical:** opaque must completely block the dark metal color. If you can see metal showing through, the layer is too thin.
**3d.** Vibrate the framework to compact the opaque, blot excess moisture with gauze.
**3e.** Fire per the manufacturer's opaque schedule (typically 940°C with vacuum, 1 min hold).
**3f.** Inspect after firing: opaque should be smooth, uniform, dark color completely blocked. If pinholes or thin spots, apply a second opaque layer + refire.
**4a.** Mix dentin porcelain (the shade chosen at the patient visit, e.g., A2 dentin).
**4b.** Apply to build the bulk of the crown. Use the diagnostic wax-up shape as the guide (referenced photos help).
**4c. Over-contour by ~20–30%** in all dimensions — porcelain shrinks 27–45% during firing.
**4d.** Brush dentin in layers, blending with cervical chroma modifiers if needed.
**4e.** Vibrate + blot to compact and remove water.
**4f.** Use the diamond burs lightly to cut back the incisal 1/3 (creates space for the enamel layer).
**4g.** Apply any internal characterizations (Mamelons, halo, fluorescent stain) at this stage.
**4h. Do NOT fire yet** — dentin is fired together with enamel in Step 5.

blocks.text: Step 5 — Enamel porcelain + first firing (45 min + 30 min firing)

items: **5a.** Mix enamel porcelain (translucent layer; typically 1–2 shades lighter than dentin).
**5b.** Apply over the incisal 1/3 and any other translucent areas (incisal edge, marginal ridges, slight cervical translucency for naturalism).
**5c.** Continue to over-contour by ~20–30%.
**5d.** Vibrate + blot.
**5e.** Fire the dentin + enamel together per the manufacturer's body firing schedule (typically 930°C with vacuum, 1 min hold, then slow cooling).
**5f. Slow cooling is critical** — rapid cooling creates thermal stress that fractures the porcelain at the metal-ceramic interface.
**5g.** Inspect: porcelain has fused into a smooth glossy surface. Color should be close to target but slightly translucent (final shade comes after glaze).
**6a.** Use diamond burs at slow speed to refine the contour: facial form, marginal ridges, cusp tips, embrasures.
**6b.** Verify proximal contacts on the working cast: floss should pass with snug resistance, contacts in the cervical 1/3 of the contact area.
**6c.** Verify margins: explorer should not catch.
**6d.** Verify occlusal contacts in MIP + excursions on the articulator.
**6e.** Verify pontic (FPD): modified ridge lap, floss passes under, no concavities.

**6f.** For 4+ unit cases: send to clinic for **bisque bake try-in** (unglazed, unstained). The dentist evaluates: interproximal → margins → pontic → occlusion → contours. Adjustments made chairside don't risk breaking the glaze.
**6g.** For single-unit cases: skip bisque bake try-in; proceed to Step 7.
**7a. Stain placement basics:**
**Yellow:** cervical 1/3 (mimics root exposure / chroma).
**Blue:** incisal edge (mimics enamel translucency).
**Brown:** interproximal grooves (mimics aging).
**White:** facial spots (mimics decalcifications, hypoplasia).
**7b.** Mix stains with glaze liquid to a thin watery consistency.
**7c.** Apply with a fine brush. Less is more — over-stained crowns look fake.
**7d.** Blend with a clean brush to soften the stain transitions.
**7e.** Build-up + characterization completes here; the next firing fixes the stain + creates the glaze.

blocks.text: Step 8 — Glaze firing (30 min firing time)

items: **8a. preferred: autoglaze (natural glaze).** Fire the bisque-baked + stained restoration to the porcelain's natural fusion temperature (~30°C above bisque bake temp). The surface fuses to a smooth glaze without applied glaze paste.

**8b. Alternative: overglaze.** Apply glaze paste over the entire porcelain surface; fire per manufacturer. Overglaze wears more rapidly in service + may erode leaving rough porous surface — not preferred.
**8c.** Cool slowly per the manufacturer's schedule (5–10 min ramp to 200°C, then bench cool).
**8d.** Inspect: smooth glossy surface, characterizations visible but subtle, color matches the shade card under standard lighting.

blocks.text: Step 9 — Metal collar polishing (PFM only, 15 min)

items: **9a.** Identify the metal collar (the band of exposed metal at the cervical margin).
**9b.** Polish with brown rubber points (coarse) at slow speed.
**9c.** Refine with green rubber points (fine).
**9d.** Final high-shine with rouge / polishing compound on a chamois wheel.
**9e. Goal:** remove the oxide layer from the visible metal, enhance esthetics, improve corrosion resistance.
**9f.** Final inspection: smooth, polished, no scratches, no exposed oxide.
**Opaque:** blocks dark metal color.
**Dentin:** bulk + most color contribution.
**Enamel:** translucency, contour.

blocks.text: Ceramic wetting on alloy surface; mechanical interlocking via roughness; chemical bond (Sn, In, Ga oxides migrate to surface during oxidation firing); residual compression on ceramic (porcelain CTE ~13.5×10-6/°C slightly less than metal ~14.0×10-6/°C — ceramic in compression on cooling).

Change in porcelain lattice from vitreous to crystalline form when fired too often. **Increases CTE and opacity.** Why repeat firings are limited — correct + refire sparingly, ideally not more than 3–4 firings total per unit.

blocks.title: Faculty checkpoints
blocks.body: **Bisque bake indicated:** 4+ unit cases — adjustments without breaking glaze, minimize refire. **Firing processes:** densification (volume reduction from particles fusing) + sintering (porcelain particles fuse at high temp). **Over-firing:** devitrification → increased CTE and opacity, compromised bond. **FPD evaluation sequence:** interproximal (floss not shred) → margins (explorer undetectable) → pontic (modified ridge lap, floss under) → occlusion (MIP even, protrusive shared, lateral excursive with disclusion) → contours.

blocks.title: Issues
blocks.body: **Opaque shows metal through:** too thin. Apply a second opaque layer + refire. Greater than 2 opaque firings risks devitrification — remake if persistent. **Porcelain has cracks after firing:** rapid cooling. Re-bisque-bake with slower cooling schedule. If cracks persist after refire, remake. **Color too dark after final firing:** over-stained or wrong dentin shade. Polish off the stain layer + apply correction porcelain in lighter shade. **Color too light after final firing:** under-stained. Add chroma modifier stain in a single application + glaze fire. **Margins lifted from metal during firing:** incompatible CTE between alloy and porcelain — should have been caught at material selection. Remake with matched system. **Porcelain fractures during final contouring with diamond burs:** too aggressive grinding or thin porcelain. Reduce in thinner increments at slower speed. **Bisque bake try-in shows occlusion is off:** adjust chairside with red-stripe diamond (porcelain is still soft at bisque stage). Send back to lab with notes for glaze firing. **Metal collar has porosity after polishing:** casting porosity exposed by polishing. Fill with cervical porcelain + refire if possible, or remake if extensive.

blocks.text: *Skills + pitfall reference that cuts across every workflow. Read once, then dip back in as needed.*

chapters.verdict: The PFM porcelain workflow: sandblast → oxidation → opaque → dentin → enamel → vacuum-fire → slow-cool. Each layer has a function. Compensate for 27–45% shrinkage by over-contouring before firing. Lab-side work; typically 2–3 hours per unit including firing time, more for FPDs.

chapters.title: Shade taking

blocks.text: **Before anesthesia / preparation.** Dry teeth read brighter — value rises when teeth are dry.

items: **Hue** — wavelength family.
**Value** — brightness. **Most critical.**
**Chroma** — saturation.
Natural sunlight varies; incandescent is red/yellow lacking blue; fluorescent is blue/green lacking red/yellow.
**Color-corrected lighting recommended.**
**Metamerism:** two colors that match under one light but differ under another — relevant when patient lives under different lighting than the clinic.
**Vita 3D Master.**
**Vita Classic:** A reddish-brown, B reddish-yellow, C gray, D reddish-gray.
**Vita 3D Bleach Guide** — for bleached teeth.
**Natural Die Material** — "stump shade" for ceramic restorations.
Identify value range first ("see degree of grayness rather than color").
Remove distractors (bright lipstick, makeup, patient bib).
Clean teeth, multiple light sources, eye level, 10 inches.
**Wet teeth + shade tab.**
<5 seconds per look.
Rest eyes on neutral gray between looks.
Confirm at second visit if possible.
**Increasing value is hard.**
Adding white stain causes **opacity**, not brightness.
Adding complementary hue lowers value (violet + yellow = gray).
**Increasing translucency decreases value.**

chapters.verdict: Shade selection before anesthesia and prep. Value is the most critical dimension. Modifying shade is harder than getting it right the first time.

chapters.title: Common pitfalls + faculty checkpoints

items: **Define prognosis:** prediction of probable course and outcome of disease and likelihood of recovery.
**Four criteria for prognosis of individual teeth:** periodontal / alveolar, restorability, endodontic, occlusal plane.
**Patient-level risk factors:** biologic, behavioral, financial / personal.
**Long-term studies:** 3.6–13.4% tooth loss vs 0–33% implant loss → preserving teeth is the long-term winning strategy.
**Type IV preferred for crowns** (high strength, low expansion, 35 MPa, α-hemihydrate prismatic crystals).
**Type III preferred for casts.**
**W:P ratio is inversely related to compressive strength.**
**Inlay:** high-noble (good ductility for burnishing, biocompatible, corrosion-resistant).
**Long-span FPD:** base metal alloy (highest yield strength + elastic modulus for rigidity).
**Grain refining elements** (Ir, Ru, Rh, Re) improve tensile strength + elongation ~30%; less effect on hardness and yield.
**Graphite pencil marking** abrades margin and contaminates — discouraged.
**Ditching the margin** = trimming away from finish line to expose it cleanly.
**Over-trimming** = poor emergence profile, overcontoured restoration, plaque accumulation.

blocks.text: Occlusion / custom incisal guide table — faculty checkpoints

items: **Semi-adjustable articulator advantage:** closer replication of patient movement than fully fixed.
**Fixed determinants (Denar / Whip Mix):** intercondylar distance, immediate side shift.
**Adjustable:** condylar inclination, progressive side shift, incisal guidance, Bennett.
**Condylar inclination set by** protrusive bite record.
**Greater condylar inclination →** taller posterior cusps.
Vertical component of incisal guidance in full-mouth rehab → taller, steeper posterior cusps.
**Under-prep #9 in a #9-#11 FPD →** lab cannot produce ideal anterior guidance, cementation may fail, porcelain fractures, posterior teeth may not disclude properly.
**Custom incisal guide table indicated:** restoring multiple anteriors, restoring canine, need precise duplication of anterior guidance.
**GC Pattern Resin:** working time 2–3 min, setting time 4 min.
**Vaseline on incisal pin and teeth:** prevents acrylic sticking and lubricates.
**Protrusive movement:** both condyles contact top walls; maxillary and mandibular incisors edge-to-edge.
**Posterior teeth prevent excessive anterior contact in MIP.**
**Anterior teeth disengage posterior teeth in all excursive movements.**
Inadequate posterior contact → flaring of incisors, diastemas, PDL widening, tooth mobility, loss of VDO, anterior chipping.
**Canine guidance:** 26% population, longest / largest root, ideal for lateral force.
**Group function:** 41% in 20s/30s, 68% in 50s/60s (canine wear). Avoid loading beyond first molar MB cusp (TMJ is class III lever).
**Lab Rx for framework:** alloy, margin design, pontic design, occlusal contact location, what was sent, return for what step.

**Margin designs PFM:** metal collar (excellent adaptation, unesthetic), disappearing margin (less visible but gray gingival hue), porcelain butt margin (best esthetics, risk of chipping, demanding lab).
**Porcelain on tissue side of pontic:** easier to adjust if heavy; metal indicated when interocclusal space limited.
**Minimum metal thickness:** 0.2–0.3 mm.
**Minimum connector height PFM FPD:** 2 mm.
**Fit Checker purpose:** identify binding areas.
**Occlusal contact location:** entirely porcelain or entirely metal; never at the junction (1.0–2.0 mm minimum from junction).
**Porcelain thickness 0.8–2.0 mm range.**
Under-reduction inside the putty even when outside looks fine (Putty Reduction Considerations).
Feather-edge margins for digital — mill burs can't fabricate, scanner can't capture.
Sharp internal line angles for digital — overmill causes thin restoration.
Subgingival margins without adequate retraction — IOS will not capture.
Bite registration when casts can be hand articulated (introduces error vs no record).
Diamond burs on metal framework (particles embed, bubble during firing).
Porcelain butt margin chipping (under-supported edge).
Saddle / ridge lap pontic — uncleansable, causes inflammation.
Over-trimming a die — bulky restoration, gingival inflammation.
Marking finish line on die with graphite pencil.
Single crowns instead of FPD for replacing missing tooth → abutments shift, no pontic site development, no trial restoration.
Cementing zirconia with HF (does nothing — no glass).
Cementing feldspathic with conventional cement (will fracture under load).
Not air-abrading zirconia intaglio before cementation.
Not following manufacturer's HF times (e.max is 20 sec, not 60 — over-etching weakens).
Adjusting glazed porcelain with regular diamond and not re-polishing.

blocks.text: Standard SOAP-format templates for the four most common indirect-restoration visits. Edit the [bracketed] fields per case. The clinic note builder can autofill basic patient info; the procedural detail below is what you add.

**S:** Patient presents for prep of tooth [#XX] for [PFM crown / e.max crown / FPD abutment]. Reviewed shade selection ([Vita A2/A3.5/etc.]) under natural light pre-anesthesia. Patient agreed to plan + signed informed consent.

**O:** [#XX] prepped with [chamfer / shoulder] finish line, [supragingival / equigingival / subgingival] margin, [1.5 mm functional cusp / 1.0 mm non-functional / 0.5–1.0 mm chamfer / 6–10° taper]. Margins verified with cord-retraction technique ([#00, #0 cords] placed for [3–5 min] with [Hemodent / ViscoStat]). Heavy + light body PVS impression ([Aquasil Ultra / equivalent]) taken in [triple-tray / full-arch tray]. Bite registration with [Regisil PB]. Provisional fabricated from [Bis-acryl / Integrity] in [shade], cemented with [TempBond NE].
**A:** Tooth [#XX] [PFM / e.max / zirconia / cast gold] crown — preparation + impression complete. Provisional in place.
**P:** Lab Rx submitted for [material, shade, occlusal scheme]. Patient scheduled for cementation in [2–3 weeks]. Reviewed provisional care (avoid sticky foods, brush + floss gently, call if loose).
Visit 2 — Framework try-in (if applicable: PFM, FPD, cast metal)
**S:** Patient returns for framework try-in. No symptoms reported since prep visit. Provisional intact.

**O:** Provisional removed without difficulty. Prep evaluated — no caries, no fracture. Cast metal framework seated passively on prep + adjacent contacts. Verified: passive seating without finger pressure, marginal continuity at chamfer, no rocking, occlusal clearance for opposing teeth in MIP + excursions. Patient confirmed no high spots. Framework cleaned + returned to lab for porcelain (bisque-bake) layer.
**A:** Framework passive fit confirmed; lab to apply porcelain stage.
**P:** Provisional re-cemented. Patient scheduled for cementation in [1–2 weeks].
**S:** Patient returns for final cementation. No symptoms reported. Provisional intact at recall.

**O:** Provisional removed; prep cleaned with [pumice slurry / Consepsis]. Final crown try-in: passive seat, marginal continuity confirmed, proximal contacts with floss (snap-through with normal resistance), occlusion checked with articulating paper (centric + excursions both balanced). Intaglio treated per material protocol ([air-abrasion / HF + silane / MDP primer]). Tooth prep treated per cement protocol ([phosphoric etch + bond / RMGI primer / self-adhesive]). Crown cemented with [cement name] under [finger pressure / patient bite] for [time]. Excess cement removed at [gel stage with floss + explorer]. Final cure 60 sec all axial surfaces. Margins verified clean of cement. Occlusion re-checked + adjusted with [football / 30-fluted carbide], polished with [porcelain / zirconia polishing kit].
**A:** Tooth [#XX] [material] crown cemented per protocol. Margins sealed, occlusion balanced, patient comfortable.

**P:** 24-hour comfort check (phone or in-person). 1-week recall for occlusion + tissue health. Reviewed hygiene + thermal-sensitivity expectations (mild sensitivity normal for 24–72 hr; call if persistent).
**S:** Patient returns at [24 hr / 1 wk / 1 mo] follow-up. Reports [no symptoms / mild thermal sensitivity resolving / specific complaint].

**O:** Crown [#XX] in place + intact. Marginal seal verified with explorer (no catch). Occlusion re-checked with articulating paper — any high spots adjusted + polished. Soft tissue around margin healthy. Adjacent contacts intact (floss snap-through normal). [If symptomatic: detail the finding + adjustment].
**A:** Crown functioning as designed. [Any adjustments documented].
**P:** Discharge to routine recall. [If issue persists: schedule additional follow-up + escalation plan].

chapters.verdict: The study-question greatest hits and the clinical mistakes that cost the most time. Pull from this before any exam, and before any chairside indirect.

chapters.title: Patient pathways — scenarios & reading order

blocks.text: **Pathway 1 — Single tooth needs a crown (conventional PFM)**
Posterior tooth needing a crown; standard conventional workflow with a lab-fabricated PFM.
**Reading order:** conventional crown workflow (read in full, all four appointments) → framework try-in (the critical reversibility checkpoint) → cementation. Material reference: PFM.
Patient wants the crown delivered in one appointment. Lithium disilicate (e.max) is the chairside default. Requires good isolation + reasonable scan-able prep.
**Reading order:** digital CAD/CAM workflow in full → cementation (the e.max-specific HF + silane + resin cement protocol is the critical step). Material reference: lithium disilicate.
Esthetic case — six maxillary anterior veneers, typically feldspathic or e.max. Diagnostic wax-up + mock-up + adhesive bonding sequence. The most technique-sensitive workflow in the guide.

**Reading order:** feldspathic + the full veneer workflow section (diagnostic wax-up, mock-up, prep with depth grooves, impression, bonding appointment with rubber dam isolation, polish) → cementation reference for the resin cement specifics → for any patient with esthetic complaints post-delivery: *CD guide * phonetics troubleshooting may apply to anterior cases that change tongue space or labial contour.
Patient missing a single molar or premolar with healthy adjacent teeth. 3-unit bridge as the fixed alternative to an implant.

**Reading order:** conventional workflow (the same appointments apply for a bridge) → FPDs — pontic design, connector dimensions, framework try-in considerations specific to bridges → framework try-in → cementation with bridge-specific section (re-cement decision tree if needed later).
Patient is planned for an RPD but a key abutment tooth lacks usable undercut or has a non-parallel guide plane. Crown the abutment to provide the design features the framework needs.

**Reading order:** survey crowns for RPD abutments in full — this chapter is specifically designed for this case → cross-reference: *RPD guide * for the RPD-side considerations → the survey crown is then cemented and the standard RPD workflow proceeds.
Tooth has completed RCT and is now restoratively ready. Cuspal protection is mandatory for posterior teeth; restorability assessment determines whether a post + core is needed.

**Reading order:** the restorability framework (front matter, "Restorability assessment + ferrule rule") → restoring endo-treated teeth (ferrule rule in depth, post + core decision triggers) → standard crown workflow per material choice: conventional OR digital.
Patient walks in with the crown or bridge in hand. Evaluate the crown + the prep + the cause of debonding before deciding re-cement vs remake.

**Reading order:** cementation comprehensive — the "Re-cementing a debonded crown or bridge" section walks through the diagnostic ladder (crown intact? prep intact? cause of debonding?). For bridges specifically, the bridge sub-section covers the three patterns (both retainers off, one retainer off = dangerous, connector fracture).
Patient with significant occlusal wear, fractured restorations, or clenching/grinding habits. Material selection becomes the dominant decision — durability over esthetics.

**Reading order:** Material selection matrix in the front matter → zirconia (3Y monolithic for posterior bruxers) OR cast gold (the most durable, least-esthetic option) → standard crown workflow → post-delivery: *build a nightguard concurrently* — the crown won't survive long without occlusal protection in a bruxer.
**Pathway 9 — Fractured cusp on a vital posterior tooth**
Patient bit on something hard, fractured a cusp, pulp still vital, asymptomatic. Restoration decision between onlay, ¾ crown, and full crown depends on remaining tooth structure.

**Reading order:** the escalation continuum decision tree (front matter, "The escalation continuum — composite → full crown") — this gives the cusp width / cusp height / occlusal contact location thresholds → onlay if cuspal coverage is indicated but full coverage isn't → conventional crown workflow if full coverage is indicated. Material per the matrix.
Patient's crown is showing signs of failure — chipped porcelain, recurrent debonding, occlusal wear-through to metal, or recurrent caries at margin. Diagnose root cause + plan replacement.

**Reading order:** cementation re-cement section with the "when NOT to re-cement" callout (recurrent debonding triggers a prep investigation) → common pitfalls for failure-mode patterns → investigate prep retention form, taper, and occlusal contact location before remaking with the same design. Consider material upgrade for the remake if the original was under-spec for the patient's bite forces.

blocks.title: How to use this page
blocks.body: Identify which pathway matches your patient. Read the linked chapters in order — each click jumps to the chapter (chapter references are hyperlinked in oxblood). Cross-guide refs (italic) require opening the named guide separately. The pathways list is a *study order*, not a procedural script — faculty checkpoints and clinical judgment always supersede the listed sequence.

chapters.verdict: Mirror of the CD and RPD guides' pathway chapters. Pick the scenario that matches your patient; read the linked chapters in order. Within-guide chapter references are clickable hyperlinks; cross-guide references (italic) point to the CD / RPD / Implants guides.

blocks.text: Cementation seats the crown at a moment when the patient is anesthetized, the tissue is retracted, and the occlusion was last verified in the provisional. The first few hours and weeks of natural function reveal whether the contact pattern matches the patient's actual MIP and excursive movements. A short 24-hour and 1-week follow-up sequence catches the contact issues before they become bruxism-induced fractures.

Occlusal contact under anesthesia is unreliable. Anesthetized patients close less precisely; the tongue and cheek positions are altered. The provisional that 'occluded fine' may have been 50–100 µm low in MIP without anyone noticing. The new crown sits in the same position as the prep allowed — and that position may not match how the patient now closes. Identifying and correcting this in the first week prevents the much more expensive problems that show up at month 3 (crown fracture, abutment pain, accelerated wear on opposing teeth).

items: **1.** Verify the patient is comfortable. Mild thermal sensitivity is normal for 24–72 hours post-cementation (residual pulp irritation from prep + cement); thermal sensitivity past 1 week warrants investigation.
**2.** Re-check occlusion with thin articulating paper (red): patient closes into MIP, identifies any 'high spot' subjectively.

**3.** If the patient identifies a high spot, locate it with shimstock (8 µm foil) — the area that holds shimstock when adjacent teeth don't is the high spot. Adjust with a fine football diamond or 30-fluted carbide.
**4.** Re-polish the adjusted area with a porcelain or zirconia polishing kit (depending on material). Unpolished adjustment surfaces accelerate opposing-tooth wear.
**5.** Re-check excursions: working-side and balancing-side. Any interference on the new crown that's heavier than adjacent natural teeth = adjustment.
**6.** Document the adjustment in the chart.
**1.** Re-verify patient comfort. Thermal sensitivity should be gone by now.
**2.** Inspect the margin with an explorer — any catch on the margin = leaky cement seal = early failure flag. Re-cement if mobile; remake if margin is open.

**3.** Re-check occlusion with articulating paper. By 1 week the patient has adapted their closure to the new crown — contacts that were high at 24 hours may have settled, OR new high contacts may have emerged as the patient stopped guarding.
**4.** Check soft tissue around the crown. Mild inflammation at the margin = food trap or subgingival cement remnant. Floss thoroughly, scale if needed.
**5.** Check contact with adjacent teeth — floss should snap through with normal resistance. Open contact = food impaction = recurrent caries risk. Re-evaluate at 6 months.
**Heavy bruxer:** verify nightguard is being worn; re-check occlusal wear on the crown.
**Endo-treated tooth crown:** verify no symptoms of cracked tooth or marginal leakage.
**Recent crown over a recently-restored core:** verify the core hasn't shifted or the crown hasn't debonded.
**Material-mismatch case (e.g., zirconia opposing enamel):** check the opposing tooth for wear; counsel patient if accelerated wear is observed.

blocks.title: Adjusting porcelain vs zirconia chairside
blocks.body: **PFM porcelain:** fine diamond OR 30-fluted carbide. Polish with porcelain polishing kit (Brasseler Dialite or similar) in 2–3 grit steps. Glaze is destroyed by adjustment — repolished surface is glossy but slightly more abrasive to opposing teeth. **Monolithic zirconia:** diamond burs only (carbides don't cut zirconia efficiently). Polish with a dedicated zirconia polishing kit (no glaze layer to worry about since monolithic zirconia is full-strength throughout). **e.max:** fine diamond, polish with e.max-specific kit. Glaze repair (paint + fire) requires lab; in clinic, polish-only is acceptable for small adjustments. **Cast gold:** carbide burs, finish with pumice. Won't fracture but will scratch easily.

blocks.title: Issues
blocks.body: **Patient reports new sensitivity at 1 week:** consider cracked tooth (transillumination + bite stick), recurrent caries (radiograph), or pulpal pathosis from prep trauma. Refer for endo evaluation if symptoms persist. **Crown debonds at 24 hours:** cementation error — likely contamination during the bonding step. Re-cement only if intaglio + prep both pass clean inspection; otherwise remake the bonding step (etch + silane + bond + cement). **Adjacent tooth complains of pressure after crown delivery:** the new crown's contact is too tight. Adjust the proximal of the new crown — not the natural tooth. **Opposing natural tooth wear visible at 1 month:** material mismatch (often zirconia on enamel without polish). Counsel patient + polish the zirconia further; consider nightguard.

blocks.text: *Where this fits in the workflow:* Cementation is the visit before this. The 24-hour and 1-week follow-ups are the bridge from delivery to long-term function. Patients who skip follow-up don't escape these issues; they just present at 3-month or 6-month recall with a fractured crown that could have been adjusted to passive contact a week after delivery for free. Build the follow-up sequence into the cementation lab Rx language so the patient expects it.*

Restoring an implant differs from restoring a natural tooth in mechanics (no PDL, no proprioception, rigid bone-implant interface), in workflow (impression coping or scan body, abutment selection, screw torque), and in retrievability decisions (cement vs screw retention). This chapter covers the procedural fundamentals — you'll encounter implant cases on every prosth rotation, even if you don't place the implants yourself.

items: **Implant body (fixture):** titanium fixture osseointegrated into bone. The two brand families commonly used at the clinic are **Straumann** and **Astra** — verify which brand the surgeon placed (it's in the patient's chart + on the implant kit).
**Healing abutment:** placed on the implant after osseointegration; removed at the impression visit with a hand driver (lefty loosey).
**Impression coping:** screws into the implant for the impression. Clinic default is **closed-tray impression** for the first implant restoration; subsequent restorations use digital scan via TRIOS.
**Custom abutment:** **titanium custom abutment** is the clinic default. Fabricated by the lab from the impression. Torqued onto the implant at the abutment try-in visit.
**Crown:** **cement-retained, all-ceramic e.max CAD/CAM** is the clinic default. Cemented to the custom abutment with **Panavia**.

blocks.text: Per the clinic implant crown delivery protocol, **cement-retained crowns on titanium custom abutments are the documented default**. Implants used are typically **Straumann** or **Astra** (verify which brand is in the patient's chart). Screw-cementable hybrid restorations exist for cases where retrievability matters.

items: **Cement-retained (clinic default):** the custom abutment is placed on the implant and torqued; the all-ceramic e.max crown is cemented to the abutment with **Panavia**. No screw access hole through the occlusal surface — esthetic outcome easier in anterior. Downsides: residual cement at the margin is the leading cause of peri-implantitis; non-retrievable without destruction.

**Screw-cementable (hybrid):** the crown is cemented to the abutment OUT OF THE MOUTH (against an implant replica), the cement-abutment-crown assembly is then screw-retained to the implant. Cement margins are out of the patient; retrievability preserved via the access screw. Documented in the clinic protocol as an option when retrievability matters.
**Pure screw-retained:** less common at the clinic for single units. Requires the implant to be angulated such that the screw access exits in an acceptable location.

blocks.text: Workflow — restoring a single implant (clinic sequence)

items: **Visit 1 — implant-level impression.** Remove the healing abutment with the hand driver (lefty loosey). Screw in the closed-tray impression coping by hand; gently tighten with the hand driver (hand-tight only — do NOT torque the impression coping to spec). For Straumann: snap the impression coping cap on in the buccolingual direction. Take vertical bitewing (or PA) to verify the coping is fully seated. Take impression with heavy body + medium body PVS (full 6-minute set; set a timer). Replace healing abutment after. Take alginate impression of opposing arch + bite registration with Regisil. Pour the impression in **Silky Rock (Type IV stone)** with the impression coping + implant replica + soft tissue moulage. Send lab Rx for **titanium custom abutment, cement-retained, all-ceramic e.max CAD/CAM crown**. Codes: DD6057A (Custom Abutment — Initial Preparation), DD6057B (Custom Abutment — Scan & Design), D0275 (BW N/C).

**Visit 2 — custom abutment try-in.** Lab returns the custom abutment + e.max crown. Remove healing abutment. Place custom abutment using hand driver; tighten slowly (hand-tight at try-in — do NOT torque to spec yet; final torque happens at delivery only after seating is verified). Take vertical bitewing to verify full seating. Evaluate interocclusal space (≥2 mm), location + width of finish line, axial reduction + taper + path of draw. Show instructor. Remove custom abutment and replace healing abutment; do not over-tighten. Codes: DD6058A (All-ceramic crown — Initial Preparation), DD6058B (Scan & Design), D0275 (BW N/C).

**Visit 3 — implant crown delivery.** Remove healing abutment. Place custom abutment. Take vertical bitewing to verify seating. Try crown on; adjust interproximal contacts first if not seating, then margins, then occlusion. Take bitewing to verify full seating + closed margins (before cementation). Evaluate esthetics. Adjust occlusion before cementation. Polish. **Cement with Panavia** using the digital crown delivery protocol (Ivoclean → IPS Ceramic Etching Gel 5% HF 20 sec → Clearfil Ceramic Primer 60 sec → Panavia A+B mix → seat → light cure). **Torque to spec** — for cement-retained: torque the abutment to the implant body before cementing the crown. For screw-cementable: cement crown to abutment OUTSIDE the mouth on the implant replica first, then torque the assembled abutment-crown to the implant body. **Astra 25 Ncm, Straumann 35 Ncm** in both cases. Fill screw hole with composite (etch, bond, layered composite). Codes: DD6058A (All-ceramic crown on abutment — Initial Preparation), DD6058B (Scan & Design), D0275 (BW N/C).

blocks.text: Critical: residual cement is implant enemy #1

Studies consistently show 75–85% of peri-implantitis cases have detectable residual cement at the implant-abutment junction. Cement that escapes below the margin during seating is biologically active — it accumulates bacteria, drives inflammation, and progresses to bone loss. Mitigation strategies: (1) minimize cement volume — a thin coat on the intaglio of the crown, not a full fill, (2) seat over a duplicate abutment first to express excess, (3) use a cement that's radiopaque so post-delivery imaging can detect residual, (4) floss the margin within 30 seconds of seating before cement sets, (5) for esthetic zones with subgingival margins, consider screw-retained.

items: **Astra abutment-to-implant: 25 Ncm.** Applied at delivery, after seating is verified.
**Straumann abutment-to-implant: 35 Ncm.** Applied at delivery, after seating is verified.
**Healing abutment:** finger-tight (no torque driver).
**Impression coping:** hand-tight only — do NOT torque to spec. Over-torquing can strip the connection or damage the implant.
**Try-in (abutment at visit 2):** hand-tight only. Torque is reserved for the delivery visit, after final seating + occlusion are verified.

**Screw-cementable assembly:** cement the crown to the abutment OUTSIDE the mouth on the replica first; the cured assembly is then torqued to the implant body at the spec above. Panavia, light-cured 20 sec per surface.
**The instructor performs the torque step** at delivery.

blocks.text: Implants are impressed differently than natural teeth. Instead of recording the tooth surface directly, an impression coping is attached to the implant body; the coping carries the position information back to the lab. The choice of coping technique (closed-tray vs open-tray) and the use of verification jigs for multi-unit cases determines whether the lab-fabricated abutment will seat correctly.

items: **Closed-tray (snap-on or transfer) coping:** screws into the implant. Impression is taken; tray is removed with the coping retained in the implant. The coping is then removed, an implant analog is attached to the coping, and the coping + analog assembly is reinserted into the impression for pouring.

**Open-tray (pickup) coping:** screws into the implant. The tray has a hole over the coping screw. After impression sets, the screw is unscrewed through the tray hole, and the tray is removed WITH the coping embedded in the impression. The implant analog is attached to the coping after impression removal.
**Scan body:** for digital impression. A scan body attaches to the implant; the IOS recognizes its geometry; the design software replaces the scan body with the implant analog for design.

blocks.text: Clinic default: closed-tray PVS, then digital scan

Per the clinic protocol, the **first implant restoration uses a closed-tray PVS impression**; subsequent implants for the same patient use a **digital scan (TRIOS)** instead. Open-tray impressions are documented in the literature for multi-unit cases but are not the clinic default.

items: **Closed-tray PVS (clinic default for first implant):** screw the closed-tray impression coping into the implant; take vertical bitewing (or PA, per instructor preference) to verify full seating; take heavy body + medium body PVS impression with full 6-minute set; after removal, the coping stays in the patient and is unscrewed; an implant replica is attached to the coping; the assembly is reinserted into the impression for pouring.
**Straumann-specific note:** snap on the impression coping cap in the **buccolingual direction** for proper engagement.

**Digital scan (TRIOS) — for subsequent implants:** a scan body attaches to the implant; the TRIOS scanner recognizes the geometry; the design software replaces the scan body with the appropriate implant analog.
**Open-tray pickup:** documented in the literature for multi-unit cases; not the clinic default.

**1.** Check the patient's chart for the surgical note listing implant brand + diameter. Go to the implant office and ask for the implant kit, impression coping, implant replica, and e.max shade guide.
**2.** Apply tray adhesive to the impression tray (use a clean brush each time; do not double-dip). Allow 5 min to dry. Apply alginate spray adhesive to opposing arch tray.
**3.** Place gauze throat pack, attach floss to the hand driver, and unscrew the healing abutment.
**4.** Screw in the closed-tray impression coping by hand; gently tighten with the hand driver. For Straumann: snap the impression coping cap on in the buccolingual direction.
**5.** Take vertical bitewing (or PA) to verify full seating. Show instructor.

**6.** Load tray with heavy body PVS. Apply medium body PVS to the gingival sulcus around the impression coping with the micro-tip. Dry the coping + adjacent teeth thoroughly with air/water syringe first.
**7.** Seat the tray. Allow the full 6 minutes to set (set a timer). Remove impression.
**8.** Unscrew the impression coping from the implant. Replace the healing abutment; do not over-tighten.
**9.** Take alginate impression of the opposing arch + bite registration with Regisil (must be poured within 15 minutes).

**10.** Attach implant replica to the impression coping (which is embedded in the impression). Pour the impression in **Silky Rock (Type IV stone)** — apply light vaseline to PVS + gingival third of analog, apply soft tissue moulage around analog gingiva, allow moulage to set, pour the stone.
**11.** Trim cast + send lab Rx specifying implant brand, diameter, titanium custom abutment, default emergence profile, cement-retained all-ceramic e.max CAD/CAM crown.
**1.** Remove healing abutment.
**2.** Screw open-tray coping into the implant. Verify radiographic seating.
**3.** For multi-unit cases: splint copings with pattern resin or flowable composite. Wait full set.
**4.** Use a custom tray with holes pre-cut over each coping screw access. Tray adhesive applied; PVS heavy + light body loaded.
**5.** Seat the tray over the copings — the screws should protrude through the tray holes. Verify visually.
**6.** After impression sets, unscrew each coping through the tray hole.
**7.** Remove the tray with the impression. The copings come out embedded in the impression.
**8.** Attach implant analogs to the copings (analogs are now external, accessible). Pour the impression.

blocks.text: When multiple implants will support a single prosthesis (bar, bridge, fixed full-arch), the impression accuracy is verified via a sectioned jig that connects all the analogs and is luted in the mouth at the implant positions, then returned to the lab to verify the master cast. This is documented in the implant-prosthetics literature for multi-unit cases; for single-unit clinic cases the closed-tray PVS impression goes straight to the custom-abutment workflow without a separate verification step.

Digital impression with an intraoral scanner is now the default for chairside crown workflows and increasingly common for lab cases. The scan replaces the impression coping + analog of conventional impressioning, captures the prep margin geometrically, and feeds directly into the chairside design software (or a digital lab pathway). Scan quality determines crown fit — a clean scan equals a clean crown; a smeared scan equals a remake.

The clinic uses **3Shape TRIOS** as the documented intraoral scanner. The workflow goes through the **UIC Dental Lab** option in the TRIOS software for crown/inlay/onlay cases milled to **e.max CAD**.

items: **3Shape TRIOS:** clinic default. Active wavefront sampling — does not require powder. Strong on subgingival margins with proper cord retraction. Strong color reproduction.

**Other scanners (general knowledge — not clinic default):** iTero (parallel confocal, no powder), Medit i700 (open-system, STL export to any lab), Dentsply Primescan (dynamic depth of field). Clinic does NOT use these for the documented workflow.

blocks.text: The documented scan sequence at the clinic: opposing arch first, then prep arch, then occlusion. Stay below 1000 images ideally, 2000 absolute maximum (erase and re-scan if over 2000).

items: **1.** Open TRIOS software. Click 'Default operator'. Search for the patient (existing) or click 'New Patient' (first scan in digital clinic).
**2.** Click 'New Case'. Click 'UIC Dental Lab'.
**3.** Select the tooth on the model. Click 'Anatomy'. Select restoration type (crown / inlay / onlay). Set material to **e.max CAD**. Select shade. Pick any delivery date.
**4.** Scan the **opposing arch first**. Maxillary: occlusal → buccal → lingual. Mandibular: occlusal → lingual → buccal.
**5.** Scan the **arch with the prep**. Same image limits.

**6.** If using retraction cords: after scanning the prep arch, use the 'Lock' tool to color everything except the prepped tooth. Click 'Trim' to erase the prep area. Remove the top (second) retraction cord intraorally. Immediately scan the prepped tooth a second time — the scanner fills in the data of the prep with the sulcus exposed.
**7.** Turn off colors to evaluate if the margin looks clean.
**8.** Verify interproximal contacts captured + adequate occlusal clearance.
**9.** Scan the **occlusion** (distal to mesial sweep) — the software auto-occludes.
**10.** Click 'Next' → 'Send' to the lab.

blocks.text: TRIOS can't see what's hidden in the sulcus. Subgingival margins must be exposed via cord retraction. The clinic uses the standard two-cord technique.

items: **1.** Pack #00 (bottom, no tail) cord soaked in **Hemodent** below the margin to control seepage + bleeding.

**2.** Pack a second (larger, size #1 or #2, leave a tail) cord above the first to expand the sulcus. Half the thickness of the top cord should sit in the sulcus; the other half visible around the tooth circumference.
**3.** Scan the prep arch first WITH both cords in place.
**4.** Use the 'Lock' + 'Trim' tools to erase the prep area from the scan.
**5.** Remove the top cord. The bottom cord stays for bleeding control.
**6.** Immediately scan the prepped tooth again. The scanner fills in the margin data.
**Hole in the scan (missing data):** insufficient capture coverage of that area. Rescan only the affected region — don't restart the whole scan.
**Double-scanned area shows blur or ghost margin:** the software couldn't reconcile two overlapping captures. Delete the overlap section and re-capture from a cleaner angle.

**Scan tracking is lost (scanner can't orient itself):** common when sweeping into an empty area (extraction socket, no opposing landmarks). Pull back to a known stable area and re-establish tracking.
**Margin renders as a blob, not a sharp line:** moisture or saliva on the prep at scan time. Dry thoroughly; re-pack cord if subgingival; rescan.
**Adjacent contact is open in the digital articulation but tight in the patient:** scan capture was off on the contact area. Rescan the contact specifically.

blocks.title: Scan quality checklist before sending to lab
blocks.body: Before clicking 'send to lab' or 'design', verify on screen: (1) margin is a continuous sharp line, no gaps or blurs; (2) no holes in the prep surface itself; (3) adjacent contact areas are smooth and complete; (4) opposing scan covers the occlusal table of the opposing tooth; (5) bite registration articulates cleanly with no obvious gaps in MIP. If any criterion fails, rescan THAT region before committing. The 60 seconds spent rescanning saves the remake. **Before cementing the provisional:** always apply **Gluma** to the crown prep — many patients are super sensitive after crown preps. Exception: RCT teeth don't need Gluma.

blocks.title: Issues
blocks.body: **Scanner overheating mid-scan (TRIOS particularly):** common in long full-arch scans. Pause, let the scanner rest 60 seconds, resume. **Software won't stitch the three scans (prep + opposing + bite):** the bite scan didn't capture enough buccal overlap. Retake the bite scan with the scanner more lateral, ensuring the buccal surfaces of both arches are visible. **Patient gags during posterior scanning:** use a smaller scanner tip (TRIOS has multiple tip sizes), warm the tip first if available, and brief breaks between captures. **Crown won't seat from the digital scan:** verify the scan didn't miss the contact area or have a smeared margin. Rescan if margin was suspect; conventional impression if multiple scan attempts fail.

blocks.text: *Where this fits in the workflow:* The scanner is the front end of every digital pathway (ind-digital-emax, ind-cad-cam-inlay-onlay, ind-zirconia-posterior, cross-anterior-implant-esthetic). After scanning, the workflow continues to design (chairside or lab) and mill or print. The scan replaces conventional impressioning entirely; if the scan fails, fall back to PVS for that case.*

chapters.title: Intraoral repair protocols — PFM porcelain chip + zirconia chip

blocks.text: Porcelain chips on existing crowns are a common walk-in complaint — patient bit on something hard, a small piece of porcelain flaked off. Decision: composite repair intraorally (chairside, 30 min) vs lab remake. Composite repairs are durable 1–3 years and acceptable for small chips. Larger fractures or occlusal-bearing chips usually need remake.

items: **1.** Examine the chip: location, size, is metal exposed (PFM substructure visible) or is the chip in the body porcelain only?
**2.** Determine repair candidacy: chip is supragingival, <4 mm diameter, not in primary occlusal contact, patient accepts the composite-color match limitation.
**3.** Isolate the area (rubber dam if possible, cotton rolls + DriAngle otherwise).
**4.** Roughen the porcelain surface with a fine diamond bur — creates micromechanical retention.
**5.** Etch the porcelain with **5% HF (IPS Ceramic Etching Gel per clinic protocol)** for 60 seconds (longer than for e.max because feldspathic PFM porcelain is denser). Rinse 30 seconds.
**6.** Apply **silane** to the etched porcelain. Let air-dry 60 seconds.
**7.** If metal is exposed at the chip area: apply **metal primer** (Alloy Primer, Z-Prime Plus) to the metal area. Air-dry.
**8.** If metal is exposed and showing through: apply a thin layer of **opaque composite** (Opaquer shade in your composite kit) over the metal to hide it.
**9.** Apply **bonding agent** (single-bottle or two-step adhesive). Cure 20 seconds.
**10.** Layer **composite** in the chip space — start with a shade-match for the body porcelain, finish with a translucent enamel shade if matching incisal. Cure each increment.
**11.** Contour to match the original tooth shape. Finishing burs + polishing rubber points.
**12.** Verify occlusion — composite repair should NOT be a primary contact in MIP or in excursions.

blocks.title: Color match — managing expectations
blocks.body: Composite will not perfectly match porcelain. Composite has slightly different translucency, slightly different surface gloss, and ages differently (composite picks up stain over 1–2 years; porcelain doesn't). Counsel the patient before starting: 'This will be very close but may be slightly visible up close. The alternative is remaking the entire crown, which is more invasive and more expensive.' Document the conversation.

blocks.text: Zirconia behaves differently from porcelain. Hydrofluoric acid does NOT etch zirconia (zirconia is not silica-based). Composite bonding to zirconia requires a different surface treatment. The chip is also less common — zirconia rarely chips because it's much stronger than porcelain, but when it does, it's usually a delamination at the porcelain-veneer-on-zirconia interface (layered zirconia) rather than a chip in monolithic zirconia.

items: **1.** Identify whether the chip is on layered porcelain over zirconia (treat as PFM porcelain repair using HF + silane) OR monolithic zirconia (different protocol below).
**2.** For monolithic zirconia chip: isolate the area.

**3.** **Air-abrasion** the zirconia surface with 50 µm aluminum oxide at 2 bar pressure for 5–10 seconds. This is the critical step — chairside air-abrasion units (Microetcher, Danville) are needed; standard prophy polish does not roughen zirconia adequately.
**4.** Clean with alcohol; air-dry.
**5.** Apply **MDP-containing primer** (Z-Prime Plus, Monobond Plus, Clearfil Ceramic Primer). The MDP molecule bonds chemically to zirconia.
**6.** Air-dry the primer (no curing needed; MDP doesn't light-cure).
**7.** Apply **bonding agent**. Light-cure.
**8.** Layer composite. Same approach as PFM repair from this point.

blocks.title: When to remake instead of repair
blocks.body: Repair is acceptable for: supragingival chips <4 mm, single-incident damage, patient accepts color/longevity compromise. Repair is NOT acceptable for: chips at the occlusal contact area (will re-fracture), chips revealing the metal substructure subgingivally (cement seal is compromised), recurrent chipping on the same crown (the original design or material was wrong — fix the cause, not the symptom), patient who wants perfect esthetic match (composite won't deliver this). For these cases, remake.

blocks.title: Issues
blocks.body: **Composite repair debonds at 1 week:** HF etch was inadequate (rinse contamination, insufficient etch time) OR silane was contaminated before application. Re-do the protocol completely; do not re-bond on top of failed bond. **Color match looks fine when wet but yellows after polishing:** composite picks up surface stain from the air-dry step. Polish more aggressively (finer grit, longer); use a glaze-like sealant (Permaseal). **Repair feels rough to the patient's tongue at follow-up:** under-polished. Re-polish with a porcelain polishing kit. **Zirconia chip recurs after composite repair:** the composite is harder than the zirconia substrate it's bonded to — the bond failed under load. Likely remake; consider going to monolithic zirconia next time (no porcelain veneer layer to delaminate).

blocks.text: *Where this fits in the workflow:* Used in repair-fractured-porcelain-pfm and repair-veneer-debond (veneer cementation includes similar HF etch + silane protocol). Patient typically presents acute; this is a same-visit chairside fix when candidacy criteria are met. Always confirm the repair holds at the 1-month recall — early debond predicts later failure.*

A resin-bonded fixed partial denture (RBFPD), known historically as the Maryland bridge, replaces a single missing tooth (typically an anterior incisor) by bonding metal or zirconia wings to the lingual surfaces of the abutment teeth. The abutments are largely conserved — minimal preparation, often no preparation at all. Indications are narrow but for the right case the RBFPD is the most tooth-conservative fixed option short of an implant.

items: **Anterior single missing tooth, typically lateral incisor:** the classic indication. Pontic supported by lingual wings bonded to the central and canine.

**Trauma case, young patient:** patient lost an anterior tooth but is too young for an implant (skeletal growth incomplete until 18–25). RBFPD as an interim solution that doesn't damage the adjacent teeth.
**Patient who declines implant (cost, anxiety, medical contraindication) and rejects RPD (esthetic, removable):** RBFPD as the conservative fixed alternative to a conventional 3-unit FPD.
**Sound abutment teeth, intact enamel, no caries or large restorations on the abutments:** the wing bonds to enamel; needs uncompromised enamel for retention.
**Heavy bruxer or known parafunctional habits:** the shear forces on the bonded wing exceed the adhesive bond strength; debond risk high.
**Posterior site:** the masticatory forces are too high for the adhesive bond; reserved for anterior cases.
**Restorations or caries on the abutment lingual surfaces:** the wing needs enamel substrate.
**Severe gingival recession or short crown height on the abutments:** insufficient surface area for adequate bond strength.
**Deep overbite where the wing would interfere with opposing teeth in MIP:** the wing must seat without occlusal interference.
**Metal-wing (Co-Cr or noble metal):** the traditional design. Cast wings approximately 0.5–0.7 mm thick. Strong, durable; metal show-through at the wing is the main esthetic limitation.

**Zirconia-wing:** more recent design. Wings milled in zirconia, often integrated with a zirconia pontic. Esthetic advantage over metal (no metal show-through); newer bonding protocols required (sandblast + MDP primer).
**Etched-metal (Maryland classic):** the original design used base metal with internal acid-etch retention features. Largely superseded by modern adhesive bonding.

**Visit 1: Records + design.** Take a conventional or digital impression of the area. Diagnostic wax-up with the planned pontic shape and wing extension. Patient approves the design. Communicate prep requirements to the lab.

**Visit 2: Prep + impression (if prep is needed).** Minimal lingual reduction of the abutments (~0.5 mm) to create a chamfer for the wing. Some designs require no prep (no-prep Maryland). Final impression after prep.
**Visit 3: Try-in.** Lab returns the bridge. Try in dry. Verify the wings seat passively, the pontic contacts the soft tissue correctly (modified ridge lap), and esthetics match.

**Visit 4: Bond.** Isolation is critical (rubber dam). Etch the enamel of the abutment lingual surfaces (37% phosphoric acid 30 sec, rinse, dry). Apply bonding agent. Surface treatment of the wing intaglio: for metal — sandblast, apply Alloy Primer; for zirconia — sandblast, apply MDP primer. Resin cement (dual-cured), seat the bridge, hold under finger pressure during cure, clean excess at gel stage.

blocks.title: Bond strength is everything
blocks.body: RBFPD success rates vary widely in the literature (60–90% at 5 years) and the variability comes almost entirely from the bonding step. Sloppy isolation, inadequate etch, contaminated wing surface, or wrong primer choice — any of these turns a 5-year prosthesis into a 6-month debond. Isolation must be rubber dam; etch must be timed; primer must match the wing material; cement must be light-cured at every accessible angle. This is not a step to rush.

blocks.text: Set expectations honestly. The RBFPD lasts 5–15 years typically. Some patients get 20+ years; some get 2 years and debond. The most common failure mode is debond (not fracture), and a debond is rebondable — patient comes in with the bridge in hand, you re-prep the bonding surfaces, re-bond. Plan for this possibility. If the patient wants 'forever' durability, recommend an implant or conventional FPD instead.

blocks.title: Issues
blocks.body: **Bridge debonds within months:** isolation was inadequate during bonding OR the abutment lingual had a restoration the wing couldn't bond to. Re-bond with stricter isolation; if a restoration is present at the wing area, the case may not be a good Maryland candidate. **One wing debonds, other still bonded:** unequal load distribution. Re-bond the loose side; counsel patient about reducing lateral force on that wing (e.g., avoid biting hard objects with the front teeth). **Wing shows through at the gingival margin (metal show-through):** unavoidable cosmetic limitation of metal wing. Consider remaking in zirconia if patient finds it unacceptable. **Pontic looks too long compared to adjacent teeth:** ridge resorption has occurred since the diagnostic wax-up; modified ridge-lap may not contact tissue. Re-design with a shorter pontic or add ovate-pontic site development with provisional first.

blocks.text: *Where this fits in the workflow:* Used in cross-anterior-trauma as a less-invasive alternative to a 3-unit FPD. Also a viable consideration in any anterior single-tooth replacement decision (e.g., as alternative to an implant during growth, or in cross-anterior-implant-esthetic if the patient declines the implant route).*

chapters.title: Crown removal — sectioning, spreader, airway protection
chapters.verdict: When an existing crown needs to come off — fractured tooth underneath, recurrent caries that's exhausted the margin, failed endo through the crown, or planning a remake — you section the crown with a crown-removing bur, split it with a crown spreader, and pull. The case turns on airway protection (Isodry over gauze), accurate sectioning that goes through to dentin, and not fracturing porcelain into the patient's airway. Source: Swade p. 70.

blocks.text: Crown removal is one of those procedures every dentist does eventually and one nobody is comfortable with the first time. The work is mechanical — section the crown buccal-to-occlusal with a crown-removing bur, place a crown spreader in the cut, rotate to split the halves apart. Most of the failure modes are about preparation: not making a putty for the provisional before starting, not protecting the airway against porcelain fragments, not sectioning deep enough to reach tooth structure (so the spreader can't catch and leverage).

items: **From sterilization:** fixed prosth kit, handpieces, radiograph kit, fixed prosth burs, **crown-removing burs (anterior OR posterior — pick before starting)**, **crown remover (spreader)**, Isodry, patient goggles, blood glucose kit.
**From locker:** blood pressure cuff, loupes / eye protection.
**In clinic:** retraction cords + Hemodent + dappen dish.

**Make a putty.** Before bringing the patient in, take a putty impression of the cast (or the existing crown if you have it) so you can use it as a provisional matrix later. Without a putty you'll be improvising a provisional chairside, which adds 20-30 minutes and looks worse.
**Get a start check.**
**Deliver local anesthetic.**

**Select a shade.** Determine the shade of the existing crown AND record it for future reference. Choose a shade for the final crown — use overhead light, ambient light, AND natural light for the comparison (different lights reveal different mismatches).
**Place cord.** Use a retraction cord as needed to better visualize the subgingival margins of the crown — this is what tells you where to terminate the section.

**Protect the throat.** Isodry is ideal — it captures fragments and protects the airway. Gauze is acceptable but more difficult to manipulate and less reliable. Do NOT skip this — porcelain fragments aspirated into the airway are a real complication of crown removal.

**Section the crown with a bur.** Use a crown-removing bur to cut THROUGH the buccal and occlusal of the crown — down to tooth structure. The cut needs to go all the way through; an incomplete section means the spreader can't get purchase later.

**Remove the crown.** Place the crown spreader in the cut and gently rotate to force the halves apart. If the buccal-occlusal cut alone doesn't release the crown, section part of the lingual surface to facilitate. If still unsuccessful, section the lingual fully. **Be careful that porcelain does not fracture into the patient's mouth** — aspiration risk.
**Take a radiograph.** Bitewing of the tooth to visualize the underlying tooth structure now that the crown is off. Decay, fracture lines, post status — all visible only after removal.
**Continue treatment.** Whatever the case demanded: excavate decay, complete core buildup, provisionalize from the putty you made in step 1, plan RCT, plan extraction, etc.

blocks.title: Airway protection is non-negotiable
blocks.body: Porcelain fragments are sharp and radiopaque, and aspiration is a documented complication of crown removal. Isodry over the working area + active high-volume suction during sectioning is the standard. If Isodry isn't available, fold gauze into a posterior throat pack and verify it's secure before starting. If the patient is supine and the crown is in the maxillary arch, the risk is highest — consider semi-upright positioning.

blocks.title: Anterior vs posterior crown-removing burs
blocks.body: Pick the right bur before starting. Anterior crown-removing burs are smaller, more delicate, and angled for incisal access. Posterior burs are stouter and cut through the occlusal of a molar crown faster. Using a posterior bur on an anterior wastes tooth structure; using an anterior bur on a posterior takes forever and burns out the bur.

blocks.title: Crown removal pitfalls
blocks.body: **Section is partial but the spreader won't catch:** the cut didn't reach tooth structure. Extend the cut deeper — the spreader needs leverage against the dentin on either side. **Porcelain fragments break loose during sectioning:** Isodry wasn't well-seated or suction wasn't aggressive enough. Pause, secure the airway, retrieve fragments, then continue. **Crown won't release even after lingual section:** check that the cement type is conventional (which releases) vs resin (which holds firmer). Resin-cemented crowns sometimes need multi-axis sectioning. **No putty made before starting:** improvise a provisional from a previous cast or use a quick-set bis-acryl over the prepared stump. Note for future: putty BEFORE patient seated.

blocks.text: **Where this fits in the workflow:** Triggered by failed endo through an existing crown (often the precursor to ind-ch32 Crown endo access fill OR a full remake via ind-ch1), recurrent caries that extends beyond a repairable margin, or a fractured underlying tooth that needs assessment before deciding remake vs extraction.

chapters.title: Crown endo access fill — composite over Vitrebond after RCT through a crown
chapters.verdict: When endodontic treatment was done through an existing crown (rather than removing the crown to do the RCT and then placing a new crown), the access cavity needs to be filled. Vitrebond goes over the gutta-percha as a base; composite fills the access. Code D2950 (core buildup including pins). Source: Swade p. 71.

blocks.text: Sometimes RCT is done through an existing crown — the crown is in good shape, the patient doesn't want a remake, the tooth needed endo, and the path of least resistance is access-through-crown rather than crown removal first. After the obturation, the access cavity is restored with Vitrebond over the gutta-percha and a composite fill on top.

items: **From sterilization:** fixed prosth kit, handpieces, radiograph kit, fixed prosth burs, crown-removing burs (anterior or posterior — kept on the tray in case the crown is failing), crown remover, Isodry, patient goggles, blood glucose kit.
**From locker:** blood pressure cuff, loupes / eye protection.
**In clinic:** retraction cords + Hemodent + dappen dish.
**Get a start check.**
**Select a shade.** Match composite to the existing crown shade. Tip: open the "Labs" tab in the EHR to see the original shade the crown was fabricated to. Use overhead, ambient, and natural light.
**Isolate** with Isodry or rubber dam.

**Remove temp filling.** Use a diamond tapered bur to remove the temporary that was placed at the end of the RCT visit. Remove sponge / cotton pellet completely. The gutta-percha should be exposed and clean.
**Check in** — instructor checks the access prep; refine until approved.
**Clean access with Consepsis.** Apply Consepsis, scrub 10 sec, rinse 5 sec, gently air-dry leaving dentin moist.
**Place Vitrebond over RMGI base.** Dispense Vitrebond (RMGI) onto a mixing pad, mix with a spatula, apply a thin layer (~0.5 mm) to cover the gutta-percha. Light cure 20 sec.

**Etch and bond.** Etch the access cavity walls: etch enamel + dentin, wait 15 sec, rinse 5 sec, lightly dry leaving dentin moist (use high-volume suction over the tooth + light quick air dry; dentin should be glossy without pooling). Apply ScotchBond in a vigorous scrubbing motion for 20 sec, air thin for 5 sec, light cure 10 sec. If contaminated: re-etch for 5 sec only, then proceed (rinse, dry, bond, dry, cure).
**Place composite.** Place in increments and cure 20-40 sec after each increment (depending on increment size). Cure the final increment for 60 sec.
**Check occlusion.** Remove rubber dam / Isodry. Check with articulating paper.
**Finish and polish.** Composite finishing burs, Shofu, Jiffy Brush.
**Check in** — instructor checks the restoration.
**Complete codes and note.** D2950 (Core buildup — including pins).

blocks.title: Why Vitrebond over the gutta-percha
blocks.body: Vitrebond is an RMGI — it bonds chemically to dentin and seals the access opening of the canal system. The composite alone wouldn't reliably seal the GP-tooth interface (composite doesn't bond to gutta-percha). The Vitrebond layer acts as the seal; the composite acts as the structural fill. Skip the Vitrebond and you get coronal leakage that re-infects the obturated canal within months.

blocks.title: Endo access fill pitfalls
blocks.body: **Composite debonds from the access cavity floor within months:** Vitrebond layer wasn't well-adapted or wasn't there. Re-do — replace with full Vitrebond + composite. **Patient returns with sensitivity:** the access wasn't fully sealed, or the GP exposed during access work has bacterial contamination. Re-treat. **Composite stains around the access opening:** under-polished. Re-polish; if recurrent, may indicate microleakage at the margin. **Existing crown porcelain chips during access prep:** diamond bur pressure was too high or the access opening too wide. Document, evaluate the chip — if small and supragingival, polish; if large or in occlusion, the crown may need remake.

parts.label: All chapters
parts.tagline: Generated content for the direct-restoration workflow and per-class chapters.

chapters.title: Direct restoration workflow — the universal sequence
chapters.verdict: Every direct restoration follows the same eight-step arc — anesthesia, isolation, caries removal, prep refinement, etch and bond, composite placement, light cure, finishing and polishing. The case-specific chapters in Part 2 modify only a few of these steps; the rest stays constant.

blocks.text: A direct restoration is completed in a single visit, chairside, without lab involvement.
